questions.prompt: Symbol raising a pitch by a half step.
questions.explanation: Sharp (♯): Accidental symbol that raises a pitch by one half step (semitone). Found in key signatures (e.g., G major has F#) and as accidentals within a measure. Three related symbols: sharp ♯ (+1 semitone), flat ♭ (-1 semitone), natural ♮ (cancels prior alteration). Enharmonic equivalent: F♯ = G♭ (same pitch, different spelling). AP Music Theory exam expects fluent reading and notation. Bach's Well-Tempered Clavier (1722, 1742) features preludes and fugues in all 24 major and minor keys, demonstrating enharmonic flexibility within equal temperament.

questions.prompt: Symbol lowering a pitch by a half step.
questions.explanation: Flat (♭): Accidental symbol that lowers a pitch by one half step (semitone). F major has Bb in its key signature. Standard accidental rules: applies to the pitch in that octave for the remainder of the measure, then cancels at bar line. Double flat (♭♭) lowers by two half steps. Enharmonic equivalent: A♭ = G♯ in equal temperament (but spelled differently based on key context). AP Music Theory exam tests accidental application within measures, key signature recognition, and enharmonic spelling - critical for transposition and modulation questions.

questions.prompt: Quality of a C-to-E interval.
questions.explanation: C to E: Major third (M3) = 4 half steps (semitones). Examples in repertoire: opening of Beethoven's Symphony No. 5 (4 notes G-G-G-Eb is a minor third drop; first phrase of 'Twinkle Twinkle Little Star' is a perfect fifth then major sixth). Major third is the defining sound of a major chord (root, M3, P5: C-E-G). Inversion: M3 inverts to minor sixth. AP Music Theory exam asks students to identify intervals by sound (aural) and by notation (written) for all 12 starting pitches. C-Eb is a minor third (3 semitones), distinct from major.

questions.prompt: Quality of a C-to-F# interval.
questions.explanation: C to F♯: Augmented fourth (A4) = 6 half steps, equal to the tritone. Enharmonic to diminished fifth (d5): C to G♭ same pitch distance. Tritone called 'diabolus in musica' (devil in music) by medieval theorists for its dissonance; avoided in early counterpoint. Bernstein's 'Maria' from West Side Story opens with an ascending tritone (A4) resolving to a perfect fifth - intentional use of historical taboo. AP Music Theory tests tritones as critical to dominant 7th chords (the F-B in G7 is a tritone, resolving to E-C in tonic).

questions.prompt: Enharmonic equivalent of C♯ major key signature.
questions.explanation: C♯ major (7 sharps) and D♭ major (5 flats) are enharmonic equivalents: same pitches, different notation. Composers usually prefer fewer accidentals: D♭ major (5 flats) over C♯ major (7 sharps). Famous D♭: Chopin Etude Op. 25 No. 8, Debussy's 'Clair de lune' (third movement of Suite bergamasque). AP Music Theory exam tests enharmonic key relationships: F# major (6 sharps) ≡ G♭ major (6 flats); B major (5 sharps) ≡ C♭ major (7 flats). Circle of fifths shows these equivalences at the bottom (6 o'clock position). Equal temperament makes these identical.

questions.prompt: Notation showing how many beats per measure and what note gets the beat.
questions.explanation: Time signature: two numbers at start of staff (after clef and key signature). Top number = beats per measure; bottom = note value getting the beat (4 = quarter, 8 = eighth, 2 = half). Common meters: 4/4 (common time, default), 3/4 (waltz), 6/8 (compound duple), 2/4 (march). Simple meters: beats divided into 2. Compound meters (6/8, 9/8, 12/8): beats divided into 3. AP Music Theory exam tests meter identification, rebeaming notes for different meters, and recognizing compound vs simple aurally.

questions.prompt: Meter 3/4 with quarter note getting one beat.
questions.explanation: Simple triple meter: 3 beats per measure (triple), each beat dividing into 2 equal parts (simple). 3/4 is the most common simple triple meter. Examples: Strauss waltzes (Blue Danube), mazurkas (Chopin Op. 6), minuets in Classical symphonies (Mozart Symphony 40 minuet). Other simple meters: simple duple (2/4, 2/2), simple quadruple (4/4). Compound triple is 9/8. AP Music Theory exam tests these classifications: count top number (groups of 2 = duple, 3 = triple, 4 = quadruple) and beat division (simple = 2, compound = 3).

questions.prompt: Meter 6/8 dividing each main beat into three.
questions.explanation: Compound duple meter: 2 main beats per measure (duple), each dividing into 3 (compound). 6/8 is the most common compound duple meter - has 6 eighth notes per measure grouped as 2 groups of 3, NOT 3 groups of 2 (which would be 3/4). Examples: 'Take Me Out to the Ball Game,' Bach's Brandenburg Concerto No. 1 (movement 1, 6/8 sections), Mendelssohn's Songs Without Words Op. 67 No. 5. AP Music Theory contrasts 6/8 vs 3/4: aurally, both have 6 eighths but different stress patterns.

questions.prompt: Dot after a note adds this fraction of its value.
questions.explanation: Dotted note: a single dot after a note increases its duration by half. Dotted half note = half + quarter = 3 quarter beats. Dotted quarter = quarter + eighth = 1.5 quarter beats. Common rhythm in 6/8: dotted quarter = 1 beat. Double dot: adds half + quarter of original (75% total addition); rare. AP Music Theory exam tests rhythmic notation in various meters, including converting dotted figures between simple and compound meters. Standard convention since medieval mensural notation (Petrus de Cruce, ca. 1300).

questions.prompt: Term for accenting normally weak beats or weak parts of beats.
questions.explanation: Syncopation: deliberately accenting weak beats or off-beats, displacing expected stress. Creates rhythmic tension/forward momentum. Characteristic of ragtime (Scott Joplin's 'Maple Leaf Rag' 1899), jazz, Latin music, and many Baroque/Classical pieces (Bach's hemiolas in dance suites). Notational means: ties across barlines, accents on off-beats, dotted-eighth-and-sixteenth patterns. AP Music Theory exam asks students to identify syncopation aurally and to notate syncopated rhythms accurately. Brahms used syncopation extensively (e.g., 3rd symphony movement 3) to disguise strong beats.

questions.prompt: Interval pattern of a major scale (in whole and half steps).
questions.explanation: Major scale pattern: W-W-H-W-W-W-H (whole-whole-half-whole-whole-whole-half). Half steps between scale degrees 3-4 and 7-8 (the leading tone resolving to tonic). C major (white keys C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C) exemplifies the pattern. Major scale is the foundation of Western tonal harmony since the Baroque era (Rameau's Treatise on Harmony 1722). AP Music Theory exam tests building major scales from any tonic and recognizing them aurally vs. minor and modes. Each major scale has a unique key signature with specific sharps or flats.

questions.prompt: Major key with three flats.
questions.explanation: E♭ major key signature: 3 flats (B♭, E♭, A♭). Scale: E♭-F-G-A♭-B♭-C-D-E♭. To find major key from flats: name of penultimate flat = key (here 'Eb' is the second-to-last flat). 0 flats=C major, 1 flat=F, 2 flats=Bb, 3 flats=Eb, 4=Ab, 5=Db, 6=Gb, 7=Cb. Beethoven Symphony No. 3 'Eroica' is in E♭; Mozart's 'Eine kleine Nachtmusik' (K. 525) is in G major. AP Music Theory exam tests rapid key signature identification, often asking 'what major key has [N] [sharps/flats]?'.

questions.prompt: Tonic of major scale containing B-flat and E-flat.
questions.explanation: B♭ major: 2 flats (B♭ and E♭). Scale: B♭-C-D-E♭-F-G-A-B♭. Pattern W-W-H-W-W-W-H confirmed: Bb to C whole, C to D whole, D to Eb half, Eb to F whole, F to G whole, G to A whole, A to Bb half. Mozart's Symphony No. 39 (K. 543) and Beethoven's Piano Concerto No. 2 are in B♭ major. AP Music Theory exam: identifying scales from accidentals or notation - critical for melodic dictation and modulation analysis. Circle of fifths positions B♭ at 2 flats counterclockwise from C.

questions.prompt: Major key signature with 4 sharps.
questions.explanation: E major: 4 sharps (F♯, C♯, G♯, D♯). Scale: E-F♯-G♯-A-B-C♯-D♯-E. To find major key from sharps: penultimate sharp + 1/2 step = key (here D♯ + 1/2 = E). Beethoven Sonata Op. 109 in E major; Mendelssohn 'Wedding March' in C major modulating to E. AP Music Theory exam tests rapid recognition: 0 sharps=C, 1=G, 2=D, 3=A, 4=E, 5=B, 6=F#, 7=C#. Order of sharps: F# C# G# D# A# E# B# (mnemonic FCGDAEB or 'Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle').

questions.prompt: Scale degree name for the 7th degree of major scale.
questions.explanation: Leading tone: scale degree 7 in major (and harmonic/melodic minor). Half-step BELOW tonic, creating strong pull toward tonic. In C major: B leads to C. Functions as part of dominant chord (V7 = G-B-D-F includes B leading to C of tonic). 'Tendency tone': resolves up by half step. Distinct from subtonic (whole step below tonic, found in natural minor: Bb to C). AP Music Theory exam labels scale degrees: 1 tonic, 2 supertonic, 3 mediant, 4 subdominant, 5 dominant, 6 submediant, 7 leading tone. Bach's chorale harmonization always resolves leading tones to tonic.

questions.prompt: Diagram showing relationship of all 24 major and minor keys.
questions.explanation: Circle of fifths: diagram arranging keys by fifths - moving clockwise adds sharps (C → G → D → A → E → B → F♯ → C♯); counterclockwise adds flats (C → F → B♭ → E♭ → A♭ → D♭ → G♭ → C♭). 12 positions cover all major keys; minor keys (relative minor) sit inside. First described by Nikolai Diletskii (1670) and elaborated by Heinichen (1728). AP Music Theory: essential for transposition, identifying related keys, and explaining modulation. Closely related keys differ by one accidental (adjacent on circle, e.g., C major to G major or to F major).

questions.prompt: Number of sharps in G major.
questions.explanation: G major key signature: 1 sharp (F♯). Scale: G-A-B-C-D-E-F♯-G. Order of sharps: F#, C#, G#, D#, A#, E#, B# (mnemonic 'Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle'). Order of flats reverses: Bb, Eb, Ab, Db, Gb, Cb, Fb (BEAD GCF). To find a major key with sharps: penultimate sharp + 1 half step = key (e.g., 1 sharp F#: F# + 1/2 = G major). AP Music Theory exam requires identifying key signatures by sight and by sound; circle of fifths visualizes sharp/flat ordering.

questions.prompt: Number of flats in A-flat major.
questions.explanation: A♭ major: 4 flats (B♭, E♭, A♭, D♭). Order of flats: BEAD GCF. Penultimate flat = A♭ = tonic, confirming 4 flats = A-flat major. Beethoven's 'Pathétique' Sonata Op. 13 features the famous A♭ major slow movement. Chopin Etude Op. 25 No. 1 'Aeolian Harp' is in A♭ major. AP Music Theory exam routinely tests counting and identifying flat keys: 0 flats=C, 1=F, 2=Bb, 3=Eb, 4=Ab, 5=Db, 6=Gb, 7=Cb. Each next flat key is a fourth above (or fifth below) the previous.

questions.prompt: Interval that 'inverts' major third to become.
questions.explanation: Inversion: M3 inverts to m6. Rule: interval numbers sum to 9 (3 + 6 = 9), and qualities flip (major <-> minor). C up to E is M3 (4 half steps); E up to C (next octave) is m6 (8 half steps). 4+8 = 12 = octave. Quality table: Perfect inverts to Perfect; Major <-> Minor; Augmented <-> Diminished. AP Music Theory exam tests interval inversion both in writing and in voice-leading analysis. Inversions matter for understanding chord positions (root, first inversion, second inversion) and counterpoint.

questions.prompt: Order of flats mnemonic: 'B-E-A-D' continues with these.
questions.explanation: Order of flats: B♭, E♭, A♭, D♭, G♭, C♭, F♭ → mnemonic BEAD GCF (read as 'bead-G-C-F'). Reverse of sharp order (F C G D A E B → mnemonic 'Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle'). 7 flats = C♭ major (enharmonic equivalent of B major, 5 sharps). C♭ major is rarely used; usually B major preferred for fewer accidentals. AP Music Theory exam memorization standard - knowing both orders allows fast key signature identification. Charlie Parker's 'Now's the Time' uses F major (1 flat).

questions.prompt: Interval of two pitches at the same letter and octave.
questions.explanation: Unison (P1): two pitches at exact same name and octave (0 half steps). Perfect unison is sometimes called 'prime' (per Renaissance theory). Distinct from octave (12 half steps), which is the same pitch class one octave apart. Counterpoint rule: parallel unisons are weak (sound like one voice), but doubling tonic notes in voice leading is acceptable in cadential figures. AP Music Theory exam tests interval recognition; identification often starts with smallest intervals. Bach chorales avoid parallel unisons between voices, though doublings at unison occur in final cadences.

questions.prompt: Minor scale that is the relative minor of C major.
questions.explanation: A minor: relative minor of C major - shares the same key signature (no sharps/flats). Found a minor third (3 semitones) below the major tonic: C → A. Scale: A-B-C-D-E-F-G-A (natural minor form). Aeolian mode of C major scale starting on A. Bach's Fugue in A minor (BWV 543), Chopin Mazurka in A minor (Op. 17 No. 4), Beethoven's 'Für Elise' in A minor. AP Music Theory exam tests relative-minor identification (down a minor third from major tonic) and parallel-minor (same tonic, different mode: C major vs C minor).

questions.prompt: Minor scale with raised 7th scale degree.
questions.explanation: Harmonic minor: raise the 7th scale degree by half step. A harmonic minor: A-B-C-D-E-F-G♯-A (G# instead of G). Creates leading tone for stronger cadence to tonic. Augmented second interval between 6 and 7 (F to G#) gives distinctive 'exotic' or 'Eastern' sound. Used in Bach harmonization for dominant chord (E major V chord in A minor). AP Music Theory exam: distinguish three minor forms (natural, harmonic, melodic) and identify aurally. Chopin Mazurkas, Bach Inventions, and Mozart's Requiem (D minor) use harmonic minor extensively for V chords.

questions.prompt: Minor scale form ascending W-H-W-W-W-W-H and descending different.
choices: Bass on root of V then on root of I (5-1 or down 5th up 4th)
questions.explanation: Melodic minor: ascending raises BOTH 6th and 7th (A melodic ascending: A-B-C-D-E-F♯-G♯-A); descending reverts to natural minor (A-G-F-E-D-C-B-A). Eliminates augmented 2nd of harmonic minor by raising the 6th too. Pattern ascending: W-H-W-W-W-W-H = identical to major except for lowered 3rd. Used for smoother melodic motion. AP Music Theory tests both ascending and descending forms separately. Jazz often uses 'jazz minor' = melodic minor ascending pattern used in both directions, modal alternative without descending change.

questions.prompt: Scale degree of the 6th note in C harmonic minor.
questions.explanation: C harmonic minor: C-D-E♭-F-G-A♭-B-C. Natural minor 6th is A♭ (not raised). Harmonic minor raises only the 7th from B♭ to B♮. So 6 = A♭, 7 = B♮ in C harmonic minor. Distinguishing: natural minor (A♭ and B♭), harmonic minor (A♭ and B♮), melodic minor ascending (A♮ and B♮). C minor key signature: 3 flats (B♭, E♭, A♭). Augmented 2nd between A♭ (b6) and B♮ (raised 7) is the signature dissonance of harmonic minor. AP Music Theory exam routinely tests minor scale-degree identification with accidentals.

questions.prompt: Mode that is essentially natural minor.
questions.explanation: Aeolian mode: 6th mode of major (white keys A to A). A-B-C-D-E-F-G-A = natural minor scale. Aeolian and natural minor are identical. Difference from minor key: in minor key with cadences, 7th is often raised (harmonic minor) for V chord; Aeolian mode keeps natural 7th throughout. Used in Renaissance liturgical music. 'House of the Rising Sun' (folk Aeolian on A minor). Modern rock and folk often modal (not strict tonal minor): 'Stairway to Heaven' intro shifts modally. AP Music Theory exam tests Aeolian identification and distinction from harmonic/melodic minor.

questions.prompt: Quality of triad built on scale degree 5 in major key.
questions.explanation: Triad qualities in major key (Roman numerals): I (major), ii (minor), iii (minor), IV (major), V (major), vi (minor), vii° (diminished). Capital = major; lowercase = minor; ° = diminished. So V on scale-degree 5 is MAJOR (C major: G-B-D = G major). Why major? Tonic, subdominant, dominant (I, IV, V) all use Major in major key, giving strong tonal pull. In minor: i (minor), ii° (dim), III (Major), iv (minor), v (minor or V Major using harmonic minor 7th), VI (Major), VII (Major) or vii° (dim using harmonic). AP Music Theory drills.

questions.prompt: Quality of triad with root, M3, and P5.
questions.explanation: Major triad construction: root + M3 (4 half steps) + P5 (7 half steps from root). C major triad: C + E + G. Intervals between adjacent tones: M3 (C to E) + m3 (E to G) = P5 from root. Acoustic basis: the major triad approximates the 4th, 5th, and 6th partials of the harmonic series (C: C4, E4 close to natural 5th harmonic 5/4, G4 = 3rd harmonic 3/2 of C4). AP Music Theory exam tests triad quality identification both in notation and by ear. Foundation of tonal harmony - I and V chords in major key are major triads.

questions.prompt: Mode starting on E in white keys, with characteristic lowered 2nd.
choices: Soprano ascends while bass descends (or vice versa)
questions.explanation: Phrygian mode: E-F-G-A-B-C-D-E (white keys on E). Compared to E minor: LOWERED 2nd (F♮ instead of F♯ in minor; well actually E minor has F♯ in natural minor? Wait - E natural minor: E-F♯-G-A-B-C-D-E. E Phrygian: E-F♮-G-A-B-C-D-E. So Phrygian has lowered 2nd compared to minor.) Distinctive 'Spanish' or 'Middle Eastern' sound. Flamenco music heavily uses Phrygian (E Phrygian). Used in 'Wonderful World' (Sam Cooke modal bridge) and 'White Rabbit' (Jefferson Airplane). AP Music Theory tests modal characteristic intervals.

questions.prompt: Triad C-Eb-Gb has this quality.
questions.explanation: Diminished triad: m3 + m3 (root to 3rd is m3, 3rd to 5th is m3, root to 5th is d5 tritone). C-Eb-Gb: C to Eb is m3 (3 hs); Eb to Gb is m3 (3 hs); C to Gb is d5 (6 hs). Highly unstable, contains the tritone interval. Symbol: ° or 'dim'. Built on vii° in major (B-D-F in C major, since B is leading tone). Diminished 7th chord (vii°7): adds another minor third on top, e.g., B-D-F-Ab. Symmetrical, dividing octave into equal parts. AP Music Theory exam tests recognizing and analyzing diminished chords aurally.

questions.prompt: Quality of triad C-E-G#.
questions.explanation: Augmented triad: M3 + M3 (root to 3rd M3, 3rd to 5th M3, root to 5th = augmented 5th). C-E-G♯: C to E is M3 (4 hs); E to G# is M3 (4 hs); C to G# is A5 (8 hs). Symmetric and unstable, divides octave into 3 equal major thirds. Three augmented triads contain all 12 pitch classes between them (whole-tone scale connection). Symbol: + or 'aug'. Rare as a stable chord; usually treated as passing or as part of altered dominant (e.g., V+). Liszt, Wagner, Debussy used augmented chords for ambiguous, floating quality. AP Music Theory exam tests recognition.

questions.prompt: Inversion symbol for IV6 in figured bass.
choices: Tonic over dominant bass, prolongs V
questions.explanation: IV6: Roman numeral IV (subdominant chord) + 6 (first inversion). In C major: IV = F-A-C; IV6 = A-C-F (3rd in bass). Subdominant chord with 3rd in bass. Common in Bach chorales for smooth bass motion: descending or ascending stepwise through bass voices. AP Music Theory exam routinely tests: 'identify chord I-I6-IV-IV6-V-V6/4-vi-vii°-I' or similar Roman+figure progressions. Inversion symbols indicate bass note relative to chord root; soprano line is independent and tested separately.

questions.prompt: Cadential 6/4 chord function.
choices: Tonic over dominant bass, prolongs V
questions.correctText: Tonic over dominant bass, prolongs V
questions.explanation: Cadential 6/4 (I6/4): tonic notes (C-E-G in C major) over dominant bass (G). Functions as embellishment of V chord - the 6 and 4 above bass resolve to 5 and 3 (V or V7). Voice leading: 6→5 (C→B), 4→3 (E→D). Sounds like delayed dominant - listener hears tension. AP Music Theory exam analyzes cadential 6/4 carefully - it's NOT really a tonic in function, but a melodic decoration of V. Common in cadential progressions: I-IV-I6/4-V-I (especially classical era), Mozart sonatas full of these. Distinct from passing 6/4 and pedal 6/4.

questions.prompt: Major mode starting on first scale degree.
questions.explanation: Ionian mode: starts on first degree of C major = C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C (the major scale itself). Ionian is the modern major scale - all major-key music is in Ionian. Other church modes start on different white-key degrees of C major: Dorian (D), Phrygian (E), Lydian (F), Mixolydian (G), Aeolian (A) [natural minor], Locrian (B, rare). Each mode has unique interval pattern and characteristic tones. AP Music Theory exam covers modes especially in Unit 8 (Modes and Form). Renaissance polyphony (Palestrina, Lassus) used modes; Bach systematized into 24 keys.

questions.prompt: Notes of the I chord in C major.
questions.explanation: I chord in C major: C major triad = C (root) - E (third) - G (fifth). C-E-G. Foundation of key. AP Music Theory exam labels I as tonic. Roman numeral analysis: I (in C: C major), ii (D minor), iii (E minor), IV (F major), V (G major), vi (A minor), vii° (B diminished). Each chord built on its scale degree using only key signature notes. Bach chorale opening usually I or vi (less commonly other chords on strong beat 1). Closing always I (perfect authentic cadence) or i (in minor).

questions.prompt: Mode based on white keys starting on D.
questions.explanation: Dorian mode: D-E-F-G-A-B-C-D (white keys starting on D). Differs from D minor by RAISED 6th: B♮ instead of B♭. Interval pattern: W-H-W-W-W-H-W (symmetric: same forward and backward). Characteristic 'minor with raised 6th' sound: 'Scarborough Fair,' Miles Davis 'So What' (modal jazz), many Beatles songs ('Eleanor Rigby' is Dorian on E). AP Music Theory exam tests modal identification both written and aural. Modes are revived in jazz, rock, folk, and post-tonal classical (Debussy, Ravel).

questions.prompt: Number of sharps in E minor key signature.
questions.explanation: E minor: relative minor of G major, shares 1 sharp (F#). Scale natural form: E-F♯-G-A-B-C-D-E. Vivaldi's 'Winter' from Four Seasons in E minor; Brahms Symphony No. 4 in E minor; Chopin Funeral March (Op. 35) third movement in B♭ minor. To find relative minor key sig: same as relative major (3 half steps up = relative major). AP Music Theory exam routinely tests minor key signatures: A minor=0, E minor=1#, B minor=2#, F# minor=3#, C# minor=4#, G# minor=5#, D# minor=6#, A# minor=7#. Flats: D minor=1b, G minor=2b, etc.

questions.prompt: Mode resembling major but with lowered 7th.
questions.explanation: Mixolydian: 5th mode of major (white keys G to G). G-A-B-C-D-E-F-G. Major scale with LOWERED 7th (F♮ instead of F♯ of G major). 'Sweet Home Alabama,' 'Norwegian Wood' (Beatles), AC/DC songs, Celtic music. Flat 7 creates dominant-7th-like sound on tonic. In jazz: used over dominant chords (G7 chord pairs with G Mixolydian). AP Music Theory tests Mixolydian recognition. Distinct from major (raised 7 leading tone vs flat 7 subtonic). Bach Brandenburg Concerto No. 4 finale has Mixolydian-tinged passages.

questions.prompt: Three-note chord built in thirds.
questions.explanation: Triad: three notes stacked in thirds. Tertian harmony (Rameau 1722). Four qualities: major (M3+m3, e.g., C-E-G), minor (m3+M3, C-Eb-G), augmented (M3+M3, C-E-G#), diminished (m3+m3, C-Eb-Gb). Inversions: root position (bass = root), first inversion (bass = third, '6' in figured bass), second inversion (bass = fifth, '6/4'). AP Music Theory exam tests building and identifying triads on every scale degree. Chord symbols: C (major), Cm (minor), C+ or Caug (augmented), C° or Cdim (diminished). Found throughout common-practice tonal music.

questions.prompt: Notes of V chord in F major.
questions.explanation: V in F major: F major scale F-G-A-Bb-C-D-E-F. Scale degree 5 is C. Triad: C (root) - E (third) - G (fifth) = C major. Same notes as I in C major - context determines function. In F major, this C major chord is the V (dominant); in C major it's the I (tonic). Function depends on key context. AP Music Theory exam tests cross-key chord identification: 'this chord in different keys.' Modulation between closely-related keys exploits common chords (pivot chords). F major and C major are closely related (adjacent on circle of fifths).

questions.prompt: Quality of ii chord in major key.
questions.explanation: Ii in major key: minor triad. In C major: ii = D-F-A (D minor: D root, F minor 3rd above D since F# would be in D major but not in C major, A perfect 5th above D). Roman numeral lowercase = minor. Common use: ii-V-I progression (classic, jazz, common-practice). ii6 (first inversion) appears constantly in Bach for stepwise bass motion. In minor: ii° is diminished (in A minor: B-D-F = diminished). AP Music Theory exam routinely tests Roman numeral case (uppercase major, lowercase minor, lowercase with ° diminished, uppercase with + augmented).

questions.prompt: Diminished triad in major key (Roman numeral and notes in C major).
questions.explanation: Vii° in major: diminished triad on leading tone. C major: vii° = B (leading tone) - D - F. B to D is m3; D to F is m3; B to F is d5 (tritone). Function: dominant substitute (contains B and F, the tritone that resolves in V7-I). Often appears in first inversion (vii°6) for smoother voice leading. Cadential use: V-vii°6-I or vii°-I. Bach uses vii°6 frequently for stepwise bass. AP Music Theory exam: vii° must NEVER be doubled at root (would emphasize the tritone). Double the 3rd or 5th instead.

questions.prompt: Quality of III chord in natural minor.
questions.explanation: III in natural minor: MAJOR triad. In A minor: III = C-E-G = C major. Why major? Mediant in minor is the relative major's tonic. Pattern in minor (natural): i, ii°, III, iv, v, VI, VII (without raised 7). With raised 7 (harmonic): i, ii°, III+, iv, V, VI, vii°. AP Music Theory exam tests Roman numeral capitalization in minor keys carefully. Note III+ (augmented) in harmonic minor: in A harmonic minor, III = C-E-G#, but with raised 7 (G#) it becomes augmented (C-E-G# has M3+M3). Real-life usage: brief passing chord.

questions.prompt: Position where 3rd is in the bass.
questions.explanation: First inversion: 3rd of chord is the lowest note. Example: C major triad first inversion = E (bass) - G - C. Figured bass: 6 (interval of 6th above bass = root of chord). Sound: less stable than root position, more 'forward-moving.' Bach uses first inversion abundantly for smooth bass lines and contrapuntal flow. AP Music Theory exam: identify first inversion by examining bass note relative to chord root; double root or 5th (not 3rd) to avoid parallel issues. Common in cadential and passing motion. Compare: root position more stable, second inversion more dependent on voice-leading context.

questions.prompt: Roman numeral for tonic chord.
questions.explanation: Tonic: chord built on scale degree 1, the 'home' chord. Roman numeral I (major key, uppercase indicating major triad) or i (minor key, lowercase indicating minor triad). Provides stability and resolution. Cadential endpoint: phrases and pieces resolve to I (or i) in authentic cadences. Tonic-dominant relationship (I-V) is the fundamental gesture of tonal music. AP Music Theory exam labels I as tonic, V as dominant, IV as subdominant; secondary functions (ii, iii, vi, vii°) labeled by Roman numeral and analyzed for function. Bach chorale endings: V-I or V7-I (authentic cadence).

questions.prompt: Symbol for 7th chord (dominant 7th) in figured bass.
choices: Soprano ascends while bass descends (or vice versa)
questions.explanation: 7 figured bass: 7th chord in root position. Full figure 7/5/3 means: 7th, 5th, 3rd intervals above bass = full chord. Often abbreviated as just '7.' For V7 in C major: G (bass) with figured bass '7' realizes as G-B-D-F (G major triad + minor 7th). Inversions: 6/5 = first inversion of 7th chord; 4/3 = second inversion; 4/2 = third inversion. AP Music Theory exam tests all four inversions of 7th chords. Bach treats V7 with characteristic resolution: 7th (F) descends to 3rd of tonic (E), 3rd (B leading tone) ascends to root of tonic (C).

questions.prompt: Note to double in I chord when no specific guidance given.
questions.explanation: Doubling root: in I chord (or any major/minor triad), most commonly double the ROOT. Distributes between 4 voices: e.g., C major chord with C in two voices, E in one, G in one. Doubling 5th is also fine (less common). AVOID doubling the 3rd in major chord (could cause parallel 5ths/8ves in voice leading). Specific rules: NEVER double leading tone (tendency tone, resolves up to tonic). In V7, don't omit 3rd (B) or 7th (F); omit 5th if needed. AP Music Theory exam rigorously tests doubling rules for each Roman numeral chord.

questions.prompt: Type of 6/4 chord with stepwise bass between root-position chords.
questions.explanation: Passing 6/4: connects two root-position chords (or first inversion) via stepwise bass. Example: I - V6/4 - I6 in C major: bass G - A (passing) - B as harmony moves C → C/G → E (I → V6/4 over A passing → I6). Wait actually classic passing 6/4: I - V6/4 - I6 has bass C-D-E (steps), but V6/4 has bass D? Let me redo: V in C major = G-B-D; V6/4 = D (bass)-G-B. So I-V6/4-I6 has bass C-D-E (stepwise). The V6/4 is passing between I and I6. AP Music Theory exam tests classification of all four 6/4 types.

questions.prompt: Term for two-voice composition with melodic independence.
questions.explanation: Counterpoint: the art of combining independent melodic lines. From Latin 'punctus contra punctum' (note against note). Foundation of Renaissance polyphony (Palestrina), Baroque fugue (Bach), and 20th-century neoclassicism (Hindemith). Species counterpoint (Fux's Gradus ad Parnassum 1725): 1st species (note-against-note), 2nd species (2 notes vs 1), 3rd species (4 vs 1), 4th species (syncopation/suspensions), 5th species (florid mix). AP Music Theory exam tests two-voice counterpoint with soprano-bass framework: third, sixth motions, contrary motion, avoidance of parallel 5ths/8ves.

questions.prompt: Vocal range names from highest to lowest in SATB.
questions.explanation: SATB: Soprano (highest), Alto, Tenor, Bass (lowest). Four-part chorale texture. Ranges: Soprano ~C4-A5, Alto ~F3-D5, Tenor ~C3-A4, Bass ~E2-D4. Common-practice 4-voice composition (Bach 371 chorales) follows specific rules: voices don't cross, gap between adjacent voices < octave (except S-A which can be 1-2 octaves above T-B). AP Music Theory exam tests SATB voice leading rigorously: identifying parallels, doubling rules, range violations. Soprano usually carries melody; bass anchors harmony; alto and tenor fill in inner voices.

questions.prompt: Motion where two voices move in same direction by same interval.
questions.explanation: Parallel motion: both voices move in same direction by same numerical interval. Parallel thirds and sixths are GOOD (consonant, beautiful). Parallel perfect fifths and parallel octaves (unisons) are FORBIDDEN in traditional counterpoint - they reduce independence between voices, making them sound like one voice (Fux 1725, Bach treatment). AP Music Theory exam rigorously tests parallel-fifth and parallel-octave avoidance. Other motion types: similar (same direction, different intervals), contrary (opposite directions), oblique (one stays, other moves). Bach chorales generally show 60-70% contrary or oblique motion - voice independence essential.

questions.prompt: Forbidden parallel motion in tonal counterpoint.
questions.explanation: Forbidden parallels: parallel fifths (P5) and parallel octaves (P8/unisons). Reasons: (1) loss of voice independence (P5 and P8 are 'hollow' acoustically, fusion of timbres); (2) historical avoidance since organum dissolved into independent voices. Hidden/direct parallels (similar motion arriving at P5 or P8 in outer voices, with leap in upper voice) also problematic. AP Music Theory exam tests parallel detection in 4-voice texture (soprano-alto-tenor-bass). Permitted: parallel 3rds, 6ths, and even 4ths (in some contexts). Modern atonal music (Debussy, Ravel, jazz) freely uses parallel 5ths as deliberate effect.

questions.prompt: Motion where one voice remains stationary while the other moves.
questions.explanation: Oblique motion: one voice holds (sustains) while the other moves. Excellent for voice independence - the held tone provides anchor, moving voice creates melodic line. Common in pedal points (bass holds tonic or dominant for many measures while upper voices move). Also in suspensions (one voice holds while harmony changes around it, creating dissonance that resolves stepwise). AP Music Theory exam: oblique motion useful in voice-leading exercises. Bach uses oblique motion frequently in choral works for SATB voices, and pedal tones are foundational in fugue (final tonic pedal often). Combined with contrary, makes counterpoint shine.

questions.prompt: Function category of IV chord.
questions.explanation: IV: subdominant function. Built on scale degree 4. Pre-dominant: prepares dominant (V), often as IV-V-I or ii-V-I progression. In C major: IV = F-A-C. Strong pre-dominant function alongside ii (both contain scale degree 4 = F, which strongly suggests motion to V). AP Music Theory exam: 'subdominant' or 'pre-dominant' function is contrasted with tonic (I), dominant (V), and others. Hierarchy: tonic stable, subdominant intermediate, dominant unstable (wants to resolve to tonic). Schenkerian analysis treats T-PD-D-T as fundamental harmonic motion.

questions.prompt: Cadential 6/4 chord resolves to.
questions.explanation: Cadential 6/4: I6/4 -> V (often -> I). Functions as embellishment of V, not a true tonic. Voice-leading: 6 (above bass) drops to 5 (B); 4 drops to 3 (D); bass stays on V. Common in cadences: ii-I6/4-V-I (Mozart sonatas) or IV-I6/4-V7-I. AP Music Theory exam: cadential 6/4 is the most common 6/4 type; the OTHER three (passing, pedal, arpeggiating) less central. Always weak placement: I6/4 on strong beat is unusual; usually on weak position before V on strong beat (creating syncopated emphasis on dominant).

questions.prompt: Chord that often appears before V in common-practice progressions.
questions.explanation: Pre-dominant chords (ii, IV): typically appear before V in cadential progressions. ii-V-I and IV-V-I are the two most common cadential progressions in tonal music. ii is more common in major-key Classical-era music; IV more common in folk, popular, and Baroque. In minor: ii° (diminished) is less commonly used; iv (minor) is preferred. Common pattern: I-vi-IV-V-I, I-ii-V-I, etc. Bach chorale phrase often has subdominant function in middle, dominant before final tonic. AP Music Theory exam: identifying pre-dominant chord and progression flow.

questions.prompt: Type of 6/4 chord with stationary bass note.
questions.explanation: Pedal 6/4: bass stays on one note (pedal point) while upper voices form 6/4 inversion. Example in C major: I-IV6/4-I has bass C-C-C, upper voices E-G then F-C (forming F major 6/4 over C bass = IV in 2nd inversion) then E-G. Or V-I6/4-V: bass G-G-G with upper voices D-B then E-C (I6/4) then D-B (V). AP Music Theory exam tests this category. Beethoven's 5th Symphony opens with extended dominant pedal. Bach Toccatas often have long tonic pedal points under chromatic harmony in upper voices. Pedal 6/4 is rhetorical/coloristic, not functional.

questions.prompt: Submediant chord function (vi).
questions.explanation: Vi (submediant): two main functions. (1) Tonic substitute - shares two notes with I (C major: vi = A-C-E shares C and E with I = C-E-G). Substitutes for I in extended tonic prolongation. (2) Deceptive resolution target: V-vi instead of V-I creates 'deceptive cadence' - listener expects tonic but gets submediant. AP Music Theory exam: deceptive cadence is a tested cadence type. Bach chorales use deceptive cadences sparingly for surprise. In minor: VI is major (in relative-major key), often used for warm, hopeful contrast. Beethoven 9th Symphony 4th movement deceptive resolutions create drama.

questions.prompt: Numbers below a bass note indicating intervals.
questions.explanation: Shorthand notation for Baroque continuo (basso continuo). Bass line written out; numbers below indicate intervals above bass; performer (harpsichordist, organist) realizes the harmony. Standard since early Baroque (~1600). Used in Bach Brandenburg Concertos, Handel oratorios, Vivaldi operas. AP Music Theory exam tests figured-bass realization: writing out chords given bass + figures. Default: blank or 5/3 = root position triad. 6 = first inversion. 6/4 = second inversion. 7 = 7th chord (with quality determined by context). Accidentals before numbers raise that interval (#3 = major 3rd above bass).

questions.prompt: Function of V chord.
questions.explanation: V: dominant function. Built on scale degree 5. In C major: V = G-B-D. Contains leading tone (scale degree 7, B in C major) which has strong tendency to resolve up to tonic (C). V-I is the most fundamental cadential motion in tonal music. V often appears as V7 (adding 7th above root: G-B-D-F), with the F (scale degree 4) tendency-resolving DOWN to E (scale degree 3 of I), and B resolving UP to C. AP Music Theory tests V7 voice leading carefully. Dominant function essential to Western tonal harmony from 1600 to present.

questions.prompt: Forbidden voice-leading interval between two adjacent voices.
questions.explanation: Parallel 5ths/8ves: forbidden in tonal voice leading. Detection: examine intervals between same two voices at consecutive moments. If both intervals are P5 or both P8 (same numeric size), it's a parallel. Example: bass C-D, tenor G-A creates parallel 5ths (C-G is P5; D-A is P5). Fix: change one voice. AP Music Theory exam: every voice-leading exercise inspected for parallels. Bach chorales: zero parallels (except occasional voice-exchange first-inversion patterns that are tolerated). Hidden parallels (similar motion to P5/P8 with leap in outer voice) also problematic.

questions.prompt: Motion where voices move in opposite directions.
questions.explanation: Contrary motion: voices move in opposite directions (one up, other down). MOST PREFERRED in tonal counterpoint - maximizes voice independence, avoids parallel 5ths/8ves automatically, creates strongest sense of two distinct lines. Fux's 'best motion.' AP Music Theory exam grading often rewards contrary motion in voice-leading exercises. Bach chorales use about 30-40% contrary motion (mixed with other types). Strict counterpoint preferences ranked: contrary > oblique > similar > parallel (some). Contrary motion also automatically prevents parallel perfect intervals - structural benefit.

questions.prompt: Tendency tone in V chord that must resolve up by half step.
questions.explanation: Leading tone: scale degree 7, located in V chord as its 3rd (e.g., G major V chord in C major contains B as 3rd). Resolves UP by half step to tonic (B → C in C major). Avoid doubling (creates parallels and weakens resolution). Also avoid leaving by leap (sounds incomplete). Particularly strong tendency in cadential V-I motion. AP Music Theory exam: leading-tone resolution is a fundamental voice-leading rule. In V7-I: 7th of chord (F in G7) also has tendency tone status, resolving DOWN by step (F → E). Both tendency tones resolve simultaneously in proper V7-I voice leading.

questions.prompt: Resolution of 7th chord 7th typically goes this direction.
questions.explanation: 7th resolution: the 7th of any 7th chord typically resolves DOWN by step. V7 → I: 7th of V7 (F in C major) resolves down to E (3rd of I). ii7 → V: 7th of ii7 (C in C major) resolves down to B (3rd of V). General rule applies to all chordal 7ths. Why? Tendency tone behavior - 7th is dissonant against root, wants to resolve to consonance. AP Music Theory exam: 7th voice leading is one of the most-tested rules. Failure to resolve 7th down is a typical voice-leading error. Some exceptions exist (transferred resolution, register changes), but default is down by step.

questions.prompt: Cadence ending with V-I in root position with tonic in soprano.
questions.explanation: Perfect Authentic Cadence (PAC): V (or V7) to I, both in root position, soprano on tonic (scale degree 1). Strongest cadential closure - 'period' ending of musical sentence. AP Music Theory exam distinguishes PAC from imperfect authentic cadence (IAC). PAC criteria: (1) root position both chords, (2) tonic in soprano of I. Bach chorales typically end with PAC. Beethoven 5th Symphony movement 4 has multiple cadential PACs reinforcing C major. Perfect cadence terminology: in some traditions used differently; APMT uses PAC consistently.

questions.prompt: Musical unit ending in a cadence.
questions.explanation: Phrase: smallest complete musical unit ending in a cadence. Typically 4 measures in Classical style (Mozart, Haydn) - 'standard phrase length.' Phrases combine to form periods, double periods, larger forms. AP Music Theory exam: identify phrase boundaries by listening for cadences. Bach chorales: phrases marked by fermatas at cadences. Common phrase structures: parallel period (2 phrases, similar melodic openings, antecedent HC + consequent PAC); contrasting period (different melodic openings, same cadence relationship). Also: phrase groups (less symmetric than periods).

questions.prompt: Allowed parallel intervals in tonal music.
questions.explanation: Parallel 3rds and 6ths: permitted (and beautiful) in tonal music. Inversions of each other: 3rd inverts to 6th. Common in Romantic-era 'sweet' harmony (Schubert lieder accompaniments). Imperfect consonances - retain voice independence even in parallel motion. AP Music Theory exam: distinguish from forbidden parallel 5ths and 8ves. Bach chorales feature parallel 3rds and 6ths between adjacent voices (S-A or T-B). NOT allowed: parallel 5ths (P5), parallel 8ves (P8), parallel unisons. Hidden 5ths and 8ves (similar motion to perfect intervals with leap) also avoided in outer voices.

questions.prompt: Cadence IV-I in plagal motion.
questions.explanation: Plagal Cadence: IV-I motion. Often called 'Amen cadence' because traditionally sung at end of hymns ('Aaa-men' = IV-I). Less driving than V-I authentic - more contemplative, conclusive. AP Music Theory exam tests cadence classification. Common in sacred music (Bach chorales, Handel oratorios), and in many popular songs ('Hey Jude' McCartney coda is IV-I plagal pattern). Not standalone cadence in tonal practice - usually FOLLOWS an authentic cadence as final affirmation. Sometimes mistakenly identified - careful to distinguish from other progressions involving IV.

questions.prompt: Phrase ending V-I but in inverted position or with non-tonic soprano.
questions.explanation: Imperfect Authentic Cadence (IAC): V-I motion, but NOT meeting strict PAC criteria. Types: (1) Inverted IAC: V or I in inversion (V6-I or V-I6); (2) Soprano non-tonic IAC: soprano on 3rd or 5th instead of tonic during the I chord. Weaker conclusion than PAC. Bach uses IAC in middle of larger phrases. AP Music Theory exam: distinguishing PAC vs IAC is foundational. Visual inspection: are both chords root position? Is tonic in soprano? Both yes = PAC; otherwise IAC. Most common IAC form: V6-I or V-I with 3rd in soprano.

questions.prompt: Cadence on V chord without resolution to I.
questions.explanation: Half Cadence (HC): phrase ends on V chord, creating sense of pause but NOT closure. Often follows pattern ii-V or IV-V or vi-V approach to V. Sounds 'unfinished,' inviting continuation. Common in middle of larger structure (period antecedent ends HC, consequent ends PAC). AP Music Theory exam: identifies HC by the pause on V. Beethoven 1st Symphony 4-bar phrases often HC-PAC pattern. Phrygian half cadence (in minor key): iv6-V where bass descends F-E (in A minor), characteristic Baroque cadential figure. Bach chorales feature both regular HC and Phrygian HC.

questions.prompt: Two phrases with HC then PAC and parallel beginnings.
questions.explanation: Parallel Period: two phrases (antecedent + consequent), each typically 4 measures, with parallel (similar) melodic openings. Antecedent ends on HC; consequent ends on PAC. Total length 8 measures. Foundation of Classical-era phrase structure: Haydn Symphonies, Mozart Sonatas, Beethoven early works. AP Music Theory exam: identifying period structure. The 'sentence' is a related construction: short basic idea (2m) + repeat (2m) + continuation (4m) ending in cadence. Mozart Eine kleine Nachtmusik opens with parallel period structure - benchmark example for analysis.

questions.prompt: Cadence V-vi instead of expected V-I.
questions.explanation: Deceptive Cadence: V resolves to vi (or VI in minor: relative major) instead of expected I. Surprise effect - listener anticipates resolution to tonic but gets submediant. Bach uses this dramatically to extend phrases (delaying real cadence). AP Music Theory exam: identifying deceptive cadence by chord resolution. Common usage: extending a piece's emotional power. Mahler symphonies use deceptive resolutions repeatedly to create epic structures. Pop music: Beatles 'Yesterday' has deceptive moves. Also called 'interrupted cadence' in British analysis tradition. After deceptive resolution, typically continues to genuine resolution PAC.

questions.prompt: Period with two phrases having different melodic openings.
questions.explanation: Contrasting Period: two phrases with different (non-parallel) melodic openings, but cadence structure HC-PAC remains (antecedent-consequent). Less common than parallel period in Classical era. AP Music Theory exam: recognize both types. Example: Mozart Piano Sonata K. 545 first movement opens with parallel period; second theme might be contrasting. The key distinguishing factor: are the openings similar (parallel) or contrasting? Both still end with the standard HC + PAC cadential pattern. Modern songwriting (jazz, pop) less concerned with parallel/contrasting distinction but does retain cadential closure.

questions.prompt: Forbidden: leading tone resolution by skip or leap (instead of step).
choices: Must resolve up by half step to tonic
questions.explanation: Unresolved leading tone: leading tone (scale degree 7) must resolve UP by half step to tonic in chordal V or vii° resolution. Skipping or leaping FROM leading tone is a voice-leading error (sounds incomplete, parallel motion problems). Exception: in inner voices (alto, tenor), leading tone can occasionally descend by skip if final tonic chord has 3rd in soprano (allowing complete triad). AP Music Theory exam: examine V-I motion for leading tone resolution. Bach chorales never abandon leading tones - they resolve up to tonic, particularly in soprano and bass voices.

questions.prompt: Rule for doubling in V7 chord.
choices: Don't double 3rd, 7th, or root unnecessarily; usually complete chord with all four notes
questions.correctText: Don't double 3rd, 7th, or root unnecessarily; usually complete chord with all four notes
questions.explanation: V7 doubling: ideally complete (root, 3rd, 5th, 7th = 4 notes for 4 voices, no doubling needed). If needed to omit a note, omit the 5th (least essential). NEVER double 3rd (leading tone - would cause unresolved doubled tendency tone). NEVER double 7th (tendency tone - resolves down). Root or 5th doubled OK if must double. AP Music Theory exam: V7 voice leading is rigorously tested. Bach chorales: V7 usually complete; if 4 voices and complete V7, leading tone in alto or tenor sometimes descends to 5 of I (when soprano takes 3rd of I) - the 'unresolved' inner-voice leading-tone exception.

questions.prompt: Maximum allowed voice crossing in SATB.
choices: Steps and skips of P4, P5, M6, octave
questions.explanation: Voice crossing: avoided in standard 4-voice texture. Soprano always above Alto; Alto above Tenor; Tenor above Bass. If voices cross, listeners lose track of voice identities. AP Music Theory exam: identify voice crossing as voice-leading error. Bach chorales: never have permanent voice crossings; occasional brief crossings tolerated when essential for voice-leading necessity. Voice overlap (next-event note of upper voice below same-event note of lower voice) also generally avoided. Modern atonal music (Schoenberg, Berg) frequently uses crossing as deliberate effect.

questions.prompt: Cadence in minor with iv6 - V where bass descends by half step.
choices: Steps and skips of P4, P5, M6, octave
questions.explanation: Phrygian half cadence: iv6 - V in minor key, bass descends by half step. Example in A minor: iv6 = D-F-A becomes F-A-D, bass = F; then V = E-G#-B, bass = E. Bass moves F-E (half step down), characteristic Phrygian-mode interval. Standard cadential gesture in Baroque slow movements (Vivaldi, Bach). Distinguished from regular half cadence by this iv6 voice leading. AP Music Theory exam: identify Phrygian HC by bass motion of half step down to V root, in minor key, with iv6 chord. Sound: melancholic, archaic, modal-flavored. Bach Concertos often use Phrygian HC in middle of slow movements.

questions.prompt: Bass voice motion typically prefers this kind of intervals.
choices: Steps and skips of P4, P5, M6, octave
Don't double 3rd, 7th, or root unnecessarily; usually complete chord with all four notes
questions.correctText: Steps and skips of P4, P5, M6, octave
questions.explanation: Bass motion: prefer stepwise motion (smooth), or leaps of P4, P5, or octave (functional/structural). AVOID: leaps of P5 or P4 followed by another large leap, augmented intervals (e.g., A2, A4 - sound awkward); leaps over an octave. Specifically: bass leaps establish chord function (root-position root motion). Bach chorale bass lines: 60-70% stepwise; remaining leaps mostly P4 or P5 between chord roots. AP Music Theory exam: bass voice analysis includes interval-by-interval examination. Augmented intervals forbidden in any voice (especially augmented 2nd in melodic minor). Diminished intervals allowed if resolved by step.

questions.prompt: Most common progression in tonal music.
questions.explanation: Standard progression: T - PD - D - T (tonic - pre-dominant - dominant - tonic). Concrete chords: I - IV - V - I, or I - ii - V - I, or I - vi - IV - V - I (longer). Foundation of common-practice tonal music (1600-1900). AP Music Theory exam: this progression is the model for analysis. Variations: I-vi-IV-V (50s doo-wop); I-IV-V-V/V-V-I (Romantic-era expansion). Modern pop: I-V-vi-IV ('Pachelbel chord progression' or 'Axis progression') - millions of pop songs use this. Bach chorales: every phrase follows some T-PD-D-T variant.

questions.prompt: Progression vi-ii-V-I uses these functions.
questions.explanation: Vi-ii-V-I: tonic substitute (vi for I) - pre-dominant (ii) - dominant (V) - tonic (I). All chords ascend by 4ths (or descend by 5ths): vi → ii is up a 4th, ii → V up a 4th, V → I up a 4th. Pattern called 'descending circle of 5ths' progression. Classical era used heavily. Pachelbel's Canon in D: D-A-Bm-F#m-G-D-G-A (variation of this). AP Music Theory exam: identify functional progression by chord-by-chord function labeling. ii-V-I is the most common cadential progression in jazz (Charlie Parker, John Coltrane standards built on ii-V-I).

questions.prompt: I-V-vi-IV is the 'Axis' progression. Examples.
choices: Numerous pop songs - 'Let It Be,' 'Don't Stop Believin'
questions.correctText: Numerous pop songs - 'Let It Be,' 'Don't Stop Believin'
questions.explanation: I-V-vi-IV: 'Axis of Awesome' progression. Examples: Beatles 'Let It Be,' Journey 'Don't Stop Believin',' U2 'With or Without You,' Lady Gaga 'Poker Face,' '4 Chord Song' by Axis of Awesome compiles dozens of pop hits all using this. Pattern: tonic - dominant - submediant - subdominant - back to tonic. Also called 'Pachelbel chord progression' (though Pachelbel's Canon is I-V-vi-iii-IV-I-IV-V). AP Music Theory exam may reference popular-music harmony. The 4-chord pattern is hugely productive because of strong tonal flow and singable melodic possibilities.

questions.prompt: Progression V-vi creates this cadence type.
questions.explanation: V-vi (deceptive cadence): V sets up tonic expectation; vi resolves it deceptively (using 'wrong' chord that shares two notes with tonic). In C major: V (G-B-D) -> vi (A-C-E) - notice C and E are shared, the change is G->A and missing B. Surprise effect for listeners. Used by Bach to extend phrases, Beethoven to create dramatic moments. AP Music Theory exam: deceptive cadence is tested. Typically deceptive cadence is FOLLOWED by another cadence (often genuine PAC) to provide eventual resolution. In minor: V-VI (capital VI is relative major chord), still deceptive.

questions.prompt: Outer voices in 4-voice texture.
choices: Soprano ascends while bass descends (or vice versa)
questions.explanation: Outer voices: soprano (top) and bass (bottom). Most prominent aurally - bass anchors harmony, soprano carries melody. Compositional approach: write soprano + bass first (skeletal counterpoint), then fill in alto + tenor. Schenkerian analysis emphasizes 'fundamental structure' of soprano-bass relationship. AP Music Theory exam: outer-voice analysis fundamental. Bach chorale exam questions: harmonize given soprano with bass and inner voices. Two-voice (soprano + bass) counterpoint is the foundation; adding alto + tenor fills in chord tones. Skill: write good 2-voice frame, then complete to 4-voice.

questions.prompt: Acceptable melodic motion for soprano voice.
choices: Each tone resolves by half or whole step in specific direction
Bass on root of V then on root of I (5-1 or down 5th up 4th)
questions.explanation: Soprano motion: mostly stepwise (smooth melodic line). Permitted small leaps: m3, M3, P4, P5. Larger leaps acceptable if approached and left by step (especially returning by step in opposite direction). AVOID: Augmented intervals (A2, A4), leaps larger than P5 in close succession, awkward shape. AP Music Theory exam grades melodic quality of soprano part. Bach chorale soprano lines: 70-80% stepwise; leaps when they occur are usually P4 or P5 between chord tones. Memorable melodies (Mozart, Schubert) feature this balance: steps for smoothness, leaps for variety.

questions.prompt: Common contrary motion pattern between soprano and bass.
choices: Soprano ascends while bass descends (or vice versa)
Bass on root of V then on root of I (5-1 or down 5th up 4th)
questions.correctText: Soprano ascends while bass descends (or vice versa)
questions.explanation: Contrary motion in outer voices: best voice leading. Soprano and bass moving in opposite directions creates maximum voice independence, automatically avoids parallel 5ths/8ves between them. Example: soprano E-F-G (ascending stepwise), bass C-B-A (descending stepwise). AP Music Theory exam: grading rewards contrary motion. Bach chorales: 40-50% contrary motion in outer voices. Critical for strong cadences: V-I often has contrary motion (soprano up by step to tonic, bass down by 5th from V to I).

questions.prompt: Rule for bass at PAC cadence.
choices: Down by step to 3rd of I
Bass on root of V then on root of I (5-1 or down 5th up 4th)
Soprano ascends while bass descends (or vice versa)
questions.correctText: Bass on root of V then on root of I (5-1 or down 5th up 4th)
questions.explanation: PAC bass: V root (scale degree 5) → I root (scale degree 1). Interval: P5 down (G to C in C major) or P4 up (G to higher C). Both common. AP Music Theory exam: identify PAC bass motion. Bach chorales: almost all final cadences have this 5-1 bass motion in root-position V-I. Strongest sense of closure. With V7-I: bass still 5-1; the 7th of V7 (F in C major) appears in inner voice and resolves down by step to 3rd of I (E). Outer-voice frame for PAC: bass 5→1, soprano often 7→1 (leading tone resolution).

questions.prompt: Mozart's most common opening melodic gesture in soprano.
questions.explanation: Tonic arpeggio openings: Mozart and Classical-era composers often open themes with tonic triad outline in soprano (C-E-G-C in C major), reinforcing key. Examples: Mozart Symphony No. 40 opening (G minor triad outline G-Bb-D in soprano accompaniment), Symphony No. 41 'Jupiter' (C major tonic chord melody), Eine kleine Nachtmusik (G major triad-arpeggio opening). AP Music Theory exam: identifying these patterns shows understanding of melodic-harmonic interaction. Bach themes also often outline tonic chord; Romantic era extends to chromatic openings (Wagner Tristan).

questions.prompt: Bass motion in I-V-vi-iii-IV-I-IV-V.
choices: Up to root of V (5th of original key)
questions.explanation: Pachelbel sequence: I-V-vi-iii-IV-I-IV-V uses descending circle-of-fifths motion (bass: 1-5-6-3-4-1-4-5, alternating drops of 4ths and rises of 5ths). Pachelbel's Canon in D (1680) made this progression iconic. Modern pop uses it ('Lover, You Should've Come Over' Jeff Buckley, 'Cryin'' Aerosmith). AP Music Theory exam: identify circle-of-fifths progressions and understand functional sequence (each chord descends by 5th to next). Bach inventions and fugues often built on circle-of-fifths sequences. Provides strong harmonic momentum.

questions.prompt: Progression with each chord function 'falling' through circle of fifths.
choices: Up to root of V (5th of original key)
questions.explanation: Circle of 5ths sequence: chord roots descend by 5ths (or ascend by 4ths). Pattern: C-F-Bb-Eb-... or in tonal key: I-IV-vii°-iii-vi-ii-V-I (root motion by descending 4ths). Highly directional - strong harmonic momentum toward tonic. Used everywhere in Baroque (Bach), Classical, jazz. AP Music Theory exam: identify in Roman numeral analysis. Schemata theorist Robert Gjerdingen catalogued these patterns. Used in extended ii-V-I jazz progressions. In jazz: the 'rhythm changes' progression (based on Gershwin's I Got Rhythm) uses cycle-of-5ths in bridge.

questions.prompt: Substitute chord for tonic (I) used as tonic.
questions.explanation: Tonic substitute: vi (in major) or VI (in minor, relative major). Shares two pitches with tonic chord: I = 1-3-5, vi = 6-1-3 (in C major: C-E-G vs A-C-E). Two notes (C and E) in common. Used to prolong tonic function in extended passages. AP Music Theory exam: identify substitute chords. Beethoven 'Moonlight' Sonata opens with C# minor chord that functions ambiguously (related to A major as vi in many analyses). Jazz harmony: vi is a common 'turnaround' chord ending phrases. Functional: vi prolongs tonic in T-T'-PD-D-T patterns.

questions.prompt: Progression I-V/V-V-I uses what type of chord.
choices: Up to root of V (5th of original key)
questions.explanation: Secondary dominant V/V ('five of five'): the dominant chord OF the dominant chord. In C major: V = G; V/V = D major (dominant of G). Tonicizes V temporarily. Notated: V/V or V7/V (with 7th, common). D major contains F#, which is NOT in C major key signature - the 'altered' note signals the secondary dominant function. AP Music Theory exam tests secondary dominants extensively (Unit 7 focus). Examples: Beethoven sonatas full of V/V approaches to V before authentic cadence; Schubert Lied 'Erlkönig' uses dramatic V/V. Function: prolongs and emphasizes V.

questions.prompt: Voice-leading direction of leading tone in V/V chord.
choices: Up to root of V (5th of original key)
questions.correctText: Up to root of V (5th of original key)
questions.explanation: Secondary leading tone resolution: in V/V (e.g., D major), the leading tone is F# (3rd of D major triad). F# resolves UP by half step to G (root of V). Same principle as primary leading-tone resolution. AP Music Theory exam tests this rigorously. V/V to V voice leading: F# (3rd of V/V) → G (root of V); A (5th of V/V, common tone) → A (3rd of V if minor V; G stays nearby); C# in V7/V → C# may move to D or by other resolution. Critical: F# must resolve UP to G to maintain proper functional logic.

questions.prompt: Two chords functioning as pre-dominant.
questions.explanation: Pre-dominant chords: ii (supertonic) and IV (subdominant). Both function to prepare V. Why? Both contain scale degree 4 (in C major: ii = D-F-A; IV = F-A-C - both have F = scale degree 4). Scale degree 4 strongly suggests motion to V because 4 resolves down to 3 (in tonic) or up to 5 (in dominant). AP Music Theory exam: ii and IV interchangeable as pre-dominant function. Classical era favors ii6 in Mozart sonatas; Baroque favors IV in Bach chorales. Pop music uses IV more commonly. Both are essential building blocks of cadential progressions.

questions.prompt: Chord ii6/5 indicates this position and quality.
questions.explanation: Ii6/5: first inversion of ii7 (the supertonic 7th chord). Bass = 3rd of chord. In C major: ii7 = D-F-A-C; ii6/5 = F-A-C-D. Figured bass 6/5: 6 (D above F) and 5 (C above F). Voice leading: 7th (C) resolves down by step to B (3rd of V). Bach uses ii6/5 frequently as smooth pre-dominant. AP Music Theory exam tests all four inversions of ii7: root position ii7, first inv ii6/5, second inv ii4/3, third inv ii4/2. Provides richer pre-dominant sound than triad. ii7 contains all four chord tones in nice voice-leading positions.

questions.prompt: 6/4 with stationary bass while harmony changes above.
questions.explanation: Pedal 6/4: bass note remains stationary (pedal point) while harmony moves above. Example: bass holds tonic C; upper voices move C-E-G (I), C-F-A (IV6/4), C-E-G (I). The IV in second inversion over tonic pedal sounds NEUTRAL because of bass pedal. AP Music Theory exam tests classification. Bach Toccatas use long pedal points (dominant pedal extending V before final resolution) - classic Baroque procedure. Brahms uses pedal points for tonal stability in development sections. Distinguished from cadential 6/4 by bass behavior.

questions.prompt: Common pre-dominant progression in minor key.
questions.explanation: Iv in minor: pre-dominant function in minor key. ii° (diminished) is less commonly used (still functional pre-dom, but harsh sound). iv minor triad (in A minor: D-F-A = D minor) is preferred. Iv-V-i is the typical cadential progression in minor. AP Music Theory exam tests minor-key voice leading. Bach 'Crucifixus' from B minor Mass uses iv prominently. Schubert 'Erlkönig' in G minor features iv extensively for dramatic effect. Modal mixture: I major borrowing iv from parallel minor (e.g., C major using F minor iv) - characteristic Romantic technique.

questions.prompt: Function of IV6 in cadence.
questions.explanation: IV6: first inversion of subdominant. Pre-dominant function. Bass = 3rd of IV (A in C major). Common bass progression: bass moves stepwise A-G or A-B (smooth motion to V root). AP Music Theory exam tests this. Bach chorales use IV6 often for stepwise bass: I (bass C) - IV6 (bass A) - V (bass G). vs I-IV-V which has bass C-F-G (leap of 4th between IV and V). IV6 creates smoother contrapuntal flow. Combined with vii°6 (vii° in first inv, common in Bach), these chords provide stepwise bass options for any harmonic situation.

questions.prompt: Most common type of 6/4 chord.
choices: Perfect 4th above bass is treated as dissonant
questions.explanation: Cadential 6/4: most common 6/4. Functions as embellishment of V (NOT as I in second inversion). Pattern: ii (or IV or vi) - I6/4 - V - I. I6/4 is on weak beat or weak position; V on strong following beat. Voice leading: bass stays on V root (e.g., G in C major); upper voices form '6/4' (the 6 = C, the 4 = E) which resolve down by step (6→5 means C→B; 4→3 means E→D), leaving V chord (G-B-D). AP Music Theory exam: cadential 6/4 is fundamental, must be analyzed carefully - some textbooks notate as V6-5/4-3 instead of I6/4.

questions.prompt: 6/4 chord with passing motion in bass.
choices: Perfect 4th above bass is treated as dissonant
questions.explanation: Passing 6/4: connects two root-position (or first-inversion) chords via stepwise bass motion. Classic: I-V6/4-I6 with bass C-D-E (stepwise). The 6/4 chord 'passes' between the two stronger chords. Sounds light, contrapuntal. Voice leading: bass step-wise; upper voices may also have parallel stepwise motion (but no parallel 5ths/8ves). AP Music Theory exam tests this category specifically. Bach uses passing 6/4 in cantatas for melodic flow. Distinguished from cadential 6/4 by metric weight (weak beat) and from pedal 6/4 by moving (not stationary) bass.

questions.prompt: 6/4 chord with bass arpeggiating chord tones.
choices: Perfect 4th above bass is treated as dissonant
questions.explanation: Arpeggiating 6/4: bass moves between chord tones of a single harmony, briefly creating a 6/4 inversion. Example: bass moves C-G-C-E (outlining tonic chord) with upper voices providing C-E-G; when bass is on G, harmony is I6/4 (5th of C major in bass). Doesn't function as cadential or passing - just bass arpeggiation. Common in compositions where bass takes melodic role (concerto bass lines, walking bass). AP Music Theory exam: classify 6/4 by function. Most rare of the four 6/4 types; if not cadential, pedal, or passing, often arpeggiating. Beethoven sonata bass passages occasionally use arpeggiating 6/4.

questions.prompt: Chord that 'borrows' from parallel minor in major key.
choices: Perfect 4th above bass is treated as dissonant
questions.explanation: Modal mixture (borrowed chords): in major key, borrow chord from parallel minor. Examples: C major borrowing iv (F minor: F-Ab-C) - uses Ab from C minor key signature. Other borrowed chords: bVI (Ab major in C major), bIII (Eb major), bVII (Bb major). Characteristic of Schubert lieder, Brahms chamber music, Romantic-era expansive harmony. AP Music Theory exam tests mixture in Unit 5 and beyond. Mixture creates 'darkness' or 'depth' in major key. Beethoven 5th Symphony movement 4 transitions from C minor to C major (use of major iv? No, the parallel relationship between C minor and C major).

questions.prompt: Why must 6/4 chords be handled carefully in voice leading.
choices: Perfect 4th above bass is treated as dissonant
questions.correctText: Perfect 4th above bass is treated as dissonant
questions.explanation: P4 above bass = dissonance: in counterpoint theory, a perfect 4th between bass and any upper voice is treated as DISSONANT (despite being consonant between non-bass voices). This is why 6/4 chords (5th of chord in bass = creating P4 with root above) require careful resolution. Cadential 6/4: the 4 resolves down by step to 3 (e.g., E → D in I6/4 → V); passing 6/4: integrates the dissonance into stepwise flow. AP Music Theory exam: explains why 6/4 chords need careful treatment compared to root position and first inversion. Originates in species counterpoint (Fux): consider intervals from bass.

questions.prompt: Most fundamental forbidden voice-leading error.
choices: All voices: avoid crossing (each voice has assigned range)
questions.explanation: Parallel P5 and P8: most-tested voice-leading error on AP Music Theory exam. Diagnostic: scan adjacent chords. If between any two voices, the interval is P5 in both chords, or P8 in both chords, parallel error. Especially obvious between outer voices (soprano and bass). Solutions: change voice (use first inversion), substitute chord, redirect motion. Bach chorales completely avoid these. Hidden 5ths and 8ves (similar motion to P5/P8 in outer voices with leap in upper voice) also avoided but less rigorously. Voice exchange (interchange of pitches between voices) sometimes excuses brief parallel.

questions.prompt: Voice that leads to tonic by half step in major key.
questions.explanation: Leading tone: scale degree 7, half step below tonic. In major key: natural 7 IS the leading tone (e.g., B in C major). In minor key: natural 7 is whole step below tonic (subtonic, not leading tone); harmonic minor RAISES 7 by half step to create leading tone (G# in A minor). AP Music Theory exam: leading tone must resolve UP to tonic, especially in outer voices (soprano or bass). Doubling leading tone is FORBIDDEN (would create parallel resolution to tonic = parallel 8ves). Bach voice leading: alto/tenor leading tones occasionally descend to 5 of I (allowed when soprano takes 3rd of I).

questions.prompt: Augmented 2nd melodic interval in harmonic minor between which scale degrees.
choices: All voices: avoid crossing (each voice has assigned range)
questions.explanation: Augmented 2nd: characteristic interval of harmonic minor between scale degrees 6 and 7. In A harmonic minor: F (b6) to G# (raised 7) = 3 half steps but a 2nd by letter name = augmented 2nd. Sounds 'exotic' (Middle Eastern, Eastern European folk music). AVOID in melodic voice leading: A2 between melodic voices is forbidden. Solution: use melodic minor (raise both 6 and 7 ascending, lower both descending). AP Music Theory exam: identify and avoid augmented intervals. Bach harmonic minor cadences: V chord uses raised 7; iv chord uses natural 6; if connecting, use melodic minor or alternate chord.

questions.prompt: Forbidden interval between two consecutive notes in same voice.
choices: Augmented or diminished intervals (especially A2, A4)
questions.correctText: Augmented or diminished intervals (especially A2, A4)
questions.explanation: Melodic augmented intervals: forbidden in tonal voice leading. Particularly A2 (augmented 2nd, e.g., F-G# in A harmonic minor) and A4 (tritone). Diminished intervals like d5 (B-F in C major) allowed if resolved by step. AP Music Theory exam: scan voice motion for augmented intervals. Solution: rewrite passage using consonant or stepwise motion. Bach voice leading: zero augmented intervals in any voice. Modern jazz and Eastern music freely use augmented intervals as deliberate effect (Klezmer, Flamenco). Tonal counterpoint forbids them for smooth, singable lines.

questions.prompt: Voice that should NEVER cross another voice.
choices: All voices: avoid crossing (each voice has assigned range)
questions.correctText: All voices: avoid crossing (each voice has assigned range)
questions.explanation: Voice crossing: forbidden in standard 4-voice texture. Soprano > Alto > Tenor > Bass at all times. Crossing makes listeners lose track of voice identities. Brief crossings (one note) sometimes tolerated for essential voice-leading; but pattern is to stay in lane. AP Music Theory exam: voice crossing is a tested error. Bach chorales: extremely rare voice crossings. Voice overlap (one voice's next note below previous note of adjacent lower voice) also generally avoided. Modern atonal music (Schoenberg) freely crosses for color and texture effects, but tonal counterpoint maintains strict separation.

questions.prompt: Rate at which chord changes occur.
choices: Acceleration (harmonic rhythm speeds up before cadence)
Jazz standards / pop ballads / cadential intensification
questions.explanation: Rate at which the underlying harmony changes. Slow harmonic rhythm: chord changes every 2-4 measures or longer (Wagner, Mahler symphonic sections). Fast harmonic rhythm: changes every beat or sub-beat (Baroque cadences, jazz turnarounds). Bach chorales: typically one chord per beat in 4-voice texture. Speeds up at cadences (sometimes 2 chords per beat for cadential V7-I). AP Music Theory exam tests harmonic rhythm awareness. Effective composition: vary harmonic rhythm for expression and momentum.

questions.prompt: Common harmonic rhythm of Bach chorales.
choices: Jazz standards / pop ballads / cadential intensification
Acceleration (harmonic rhythm speeds up before cadence)
questions.explanation: Bach chorales: typically one chord per beat (in 4/4: 4 chords per measure). Speeds up at cadences (2 chords per beat sometimes, for V7-I at cadence). Slower harmonic rhythm in chorales (every 2 beats or whole-measure) would lose pedagogical density. AP Music Theory exam: Bach chorale style is the model for voice-leading exercises. Bach's 371 chorales (published posthumously, Riemenschneider 1941): density of 1 chord per beat allows demonstration of all voice-leading patterns within tight space. Faster than typical Romantic harmonic rhythm; slower than jazz turnaround.

questions.prompt: Slower harmonic rhythm tends to characterize this style.
choices: Jazz standards / pop ballads / cadential intensification
questions.explanation: Romantic harmonic rhythm: slow. Wagner operas often hold single chord for many measures (Tristan Prelude extended harmonies). Mahler symphonies feature long stretches of single harmony with melodic activity above. Bruckner: similarly slow chord changes, building monumental harmonic vistas. AP Music Theory exam may reference these stylistic differences. Reasons: emotional intensity, extended development, distance from cadential focus. Contrasts with Classical era (Mozart): faster harmonic rhythm, more cadences per minute. Form follows harmony - Romantic forms inherently larger.

questions.prompt: Faster harmonic rhythm than chorales is characteristic of this style.
choices: Jazz standards / pop ballads / cadential intensification
questions.correctText: Jazz standards / pop ballads / cadential intensification
questions.explanation: Jazz harmonic rhythm: typically faster than tonal classical, especially during 'changes' (chord progressions). Standard ii-V-I cadential motion can occupy 2 beats (ii on beat 1, V on beat 3 in cut time) or just 1 beat (in fast tempos). Bebop standards (Charlie Parker) have rapid chord changes. Compared to Baroque chorale's 1 chord per beat. AP Music Theory exam: jazz harmonic rhythm vs classical. Trad. ballad harmonic rhythm: 1 chord per measure (slower). Modern pop: variable - intro/verse slower, chorus faster harmonic rhythm with more changes.

questions.prompt: Cadential intensification typically involves this change in harmonic rhythm.
choices: Don't double 3rd, 7th, or root unnecessarily; usually complete chord with all four notes
Acceleration (harmonic rhythm speeds up before cadence)
questions.correctText: Acceleration (harmonic rhythm speeds up before cadence)
questions.explanation: Cadential intensification: harmonic rhythm accelerates approaching cadence. Common technique: 1 chord per measure for several measures, then 2 chords per measure for 1 measure, then 4 chords per measure for last cadence. Bach example: stately opening with slow harmonic rhythm, intensifying to V7-I at cadence with fast chord changes. AP Music Theory exam: identify cadential intensification. Beethoven sonatas: typical structure has slow harmonic rhythm in development, faster at cadence approaching recapitulation. Schubert and Romantic composers: more variable, but cadential acceleration remains common.

questions.prompt: Note that is not part of the underlying harmony.
questions.explanation: Non-chord tones (NCTs): notes outside the underlying chord, creating melodic embellishment and dissonance. Categories: passing tones, neighbor tones, suspensions, appoggiaturas, anticipations, escape tones, retardations. Different roles in voice leading. AP Music Theory exam tests NCT identification rigorously in Unit 6. Bach chorales feature many NCTs adding rhythmic and melodic interest. Without NCTs, music would be block-chord and dull. With them, melodies sing and counterpoint flows. Each NCT type has specific approach/resolution pattern that defines it.

questions.prompt: NCT approached by step and left by step in same direction.
questions.explanation: Passing tone (PT): approached by step, left by step in SAME direction. Connects two chord tones (filling gap of 3rd between them). Example: in C major chord (C-E-G), if melody goes C-D-E, the D is a passing tone (approached up-step from C, left up-step to E). Most common NCT. AP Music Theory exam: PT identification. Can be diatonic (D in C major) or chromatic (e.g., D# passing between D and E within E major). Bach uses many passing tones for smooth melodic motion. Categorized by accented vs unaccented placement.

questions.prompt: NCT approached by step and returning to same pitch.
questions.explanation: Neighbor tone (NT): approached by step, returning to original pitch by step (in opposite direction). Upper neighbor: chord tone-NT above-back to chord tone (C-D-C). Lower neighbor: chord tone-NT below-back (C-B-C). Forms an embellishment around chord tone. AP Music Theory exam: NT vs PT distinction (NT returns; PT continues). Bach chorales feature both diatonic and chromatic neighbor tones. Chopin Mazurkas and Brahms intermezzi use chromatic neighbor figures extensively for chromatic color. Double neighbor: upper + lower neighbor surrounding chord tone (C-D-B-C).

questions.prompt: Melodic motion outlining a chord through its tones.
questions.explanation: Arpeggiation: melodic line outlining notes of a chord in succession. Example: C-E-G outlines C major triad. Italian 'arpeggio' = 'like a harp.' Used to outline harmony melodically. AP Music Theory exam: identify chord-outlining figures. Bach Cello Suites' Prelude in G major opens with arpeggiation. Mozart piano sonatas: left-hand Alberti bass = arpeggiated triadic figures (bass-fifth-third-fifth, e.g., C-G-E-G). Chopin Nocturnes feature arpeggiated accompaniment. Concept fundamental to harmonic-melodic interaction - any chord can be 'sung' as arpeggio.

questions.prompt: NCT prepared as consonance, then becomes dissonant, then resolves down.
questions.explanation: Suspension: a NCT prepared by a previous chord tone (now consonant), TIED or repeated into the next chord (where it becomes dissonant), then resolves DOWN by step. Three steps: preparation - suspension - resolution. Common types named by intervals: 4-3 suspension (4th above bass suspends to 3rd), 7-6 suspension, 9-8 suspension. AP Music Theory exam tests suspension identification and proper resolution. Bach uses 4-3 suspensions in cadential V chord routinely. Suspensions create powerful expressive moments - the dissonance resolves with tension release. Different from appoggiatura (suspension prepared; appoggiatura not).

questions.prompt: NCT that arrives early - sounds part of next chord before chord change.
questions.explanation: Anticipation: NCT that 'anticipates' the next chord tone - arrives early. Approached usually by step (sometimes leap), held or repeated into the next chord where it becomes consonant. Opposite of suspension: suspension lingers from previous; anticipation arrives early from next. AP Music Theory exam tests anticipations. Common at cadences: scale degree 1 anticipated as final note before V chord resolves to I. Bach chorale cadences often feature soprano anticipation in last beat. Subtle expressive effect - 'eager' arrival at goal. Different from passing tone (anticipation belongs to NEXT chord; PT belongs to NEITHER chord).

questions.prompt: NCT approached by step and left by leap.
questions.explanation: Escape tone (échappée): approached by step (usually up), left by leap (usually down). The 'wrong direction' departure: melody goes up by step to NCT, then 'escapes' by leap (away from chord tone). AP Music Theory exam tests this less common NCT. Often a tiny figure: chord tone (C) → up-step to NCT (D) → leap down to next chord tone (G in V chord). Used for embellishment without strong harmonic implication. Bach uses échappées sparingly; appears in ornamental writing. French Baroque ornaments traditions formalized these patterns.

questions.prompt: NCT in cadential motion, retaining a tone for unexpected ascending step.
questions.explanation: Retardation: like suspension but resolves UP instead of down. Prepared (held over from previous chord), creates dissonance in current chord, resolves UP by step.
