Less common than suspension. Often at final cadence: leading tone retarded slightly, then ascending to tonic. AP Music Theory exam may distinguish suspension (downward resolution) vs retardation (upward resolution). Bach uses retardations particularly in slower movements for delicate expressive effect. Common pattern: 7-8 retardation = leading tone tied over to next chord where it becomes 7th, then resolves up to tonic.

questions.prompt: Type of NCT decoration with rapid alternation between chord tone and neighbor.
questions.explanation: Trill: rapid alternation between chord tone and upper neighbor. Baroque trills usually start on upper note (the neighbor); Classical and later, often start on lower (chord tone). Symbol: 'tr' or wavy line above note. AP Music Theory exam may reference ornamental notation. Trills decorate cadences and sustained notes for expressive intensity. Bach trills: Baroque style starting on upper neighbor; Mozart trills: starting on principal note. Trills always end with turn (Nachschlag) into chord tone. Different from tremolo (rapid alternation of two different pitches or repetition of same note).

questions.prompt: Decorative figure: turn around a principal note (above, principal, below, principal).
questions.explanation: Turn (gruppetto): ornamental figure of 4 notes - upper neighbor, principal note, lower neighbor, principal note (e.g., C: D-C-B-C). Italian 'gruppetto.' Symbol: small 'S' on its side. AP Music Theory exam may reference ornaments. Bach, Mozart, Chopin use turns extensively. Beethoven Pathétique Sonata uses turns in slow movement. Inverted turn: starts with lower neighbor (B-C-D-C). Mordent: shorter version - principal note, lower neighbor, principal note (C-B-C, like mini-trill). Upper mordent: principal-upper-principal (C-D-C). All ornaments tested for identification in AP Music Theory exam.

questions.prompt: Smallest recognizable musical unit.
questions.explanation: Motive (motif): smallest recognizable musical idea - typically 2-7 notes with distinctive rhythmic and melodic shape. Examples: Beethoven 5th Symphony's 4-note motive (G-G-G-Eb, short-short-short-long); Bach Fugue subjects; jazz licks. Differs from theme (longer, more complete melodic idea). AP Music Theory exam tests motivic identification and development. Schoenberg systematized 'developing variation' - composing entire pieces from a single germinal motive. Mahler symphonies expand short motives into vast structures. Motivic unity creates coherence; motivic variety creates interest.

questions.prompt: Motive technique that reverses melodic direction.
questions.explanation: Inversion (motivic): reverse all interval directions. Original ascending major 3rd becomes descending major 3rd; original up-step becomes down-step. Different from interval inversion (different concept) and chord inversion (different concept) - here, MELODIC inversion of a motive. Bach Fugue uses thematic inversion extensively (e.g., Art of Fugue with subject + inverted subject). AP Music Theory exam tests motivic transformation. Example: original motive C-E-G (up M3 up m3); inverted C-Ab-F (down M3 down m3). Inversion preserves intervallic content but reverses contour.

questions.prompt: Motive technique that reverses the order of notes.
questions.explanation: Retrograde: reverse the order of notes in time. C-E-G becomes G-E-C. Less common than transposition or inversion but used by Bach (canons with retrograde), Schoenberg (12-tone technique uses retrograde rows), Webern (intricate retrograde structures). AP Music Theory exam tests these transformations. Combined: retrograde inversion = reverse order AND invert intervals. Schoenberg's 12-tone serialism uses 4 forms of a row: prime, retrograde, inversion, retrograde inversion. Bach's Musical Offering features canonic pieces where second voice plays retrograde of first. Difficult to recognize aurally but visible in score.

questions.prompt: NCT approached by leap and resolved by step.
questions.explanation: Appoggiatura: approached by LEAP, resolved by STEP (usually down). Unprepared dissonance - sudden 'leaning' into dissonance. Italian 'appoggiare' = 'to lean.' Often on strong beat for emphasis. AP Music Theory exam: distinguishes from suspension (prepared) by approach (leap vs tie/preparation). Examples: Mozart Symphony No. 40 opening - the famous 'G-Eb' two-note motive features Eb as appoggiatura over D-Bb harmony, resolving Eb→D. Chopin Nocturnes and Schubert songs use appoggiaturas for expressive 'sighs.' Common in 18th-century 'galant' style and 19th-century song writing.

questions.prompt: Motive expansion technique using longer rhythmic values.
questions.explanation: Augmentation: lengthen rhythmic values of motive (typically double). Original quarter notes become half notes; eighth notes become quarter notes. Used for emphasis, climactic statement, contrapuntal interest. Bach fugues often state subject in augmentation near end. Stretto (close imitation) with augmentation creates dense climactic texture. AP Music Theory exam tests rhythmic transformations. Opposite: diminution = shorten rhythmic values (halving). Combined transformations: augmented retrograde, diminished inversion, etc. Each transformation reveals different aspects of motivic content. Schoenberg formalized as 'developing variation.'.

questions.prompt: Strong note approached by larger jump than just a step.
questions.explanation: Leap (skip): melodic motion of 3rd or larger (vs step of 2nd or smaller). Steps create smooth melodic flow; leaps create variety and outline harmony. AP Music Theory exam: balance of steps and leaps is fundamental melodic principle. Most melodies are 60-80% stepwise (singable). Bach melodies have controlled leap distribution: most leaps within tonic triad, large leaps usually followed by stepwise motion in opposite direction (returning by step after large leap). Rule: after large leap (octave, P5), melody should reverse direction by step. Mozart and Schubert songs balance steps and leaps elegantly.

questions.prompt: Repetition of motive at different pitch level.
questions.explanation: Sequence: repetition of melodic/harmonic pattern at different pitch levels (transposed). Can be diatonic (within key) or chromatic (modulating). Strong sense of momentum - listener expects continued sequencing. Examples: Bach Brandenburg Concertos have many sequences; Vivaldi 'Four Seasons' uses descending sequences for dramatic effect. AP Music Theory exam: identify sequences and their type (diatonic, chromatic; ascending, descending). Sequence levels usually move by 2nd or 3rd. Famous: Pachelbel's Canon (circle-of-5ths sequence). Sequence is a fundamental compositional technique from Baroque to Romantic era.

questions.prompt: Highest point of a melodic line.
questions.explanation: Melodic climax: highest pitch in melodic line. Usually occurs near 60-70% through the line (Golden Section, ~0.618). Provides melodic shape and direction. Climax should appear ONCE per melodic line for maximum effect. AP Music Theory exam: analyze melodic shape including climax location. Bach chorale soprano: usually one climax per phrase, placed dramatically. Modern songwriting: climactic note in chorus high range. After climax, melody descends to closure. Beethoven melodies often have late climax for dramatic build. Schubert lieder melodies feature lyrical climaxes that audience anticipates.

questions.prompt: Stepwise descending motion in melodic style.
questions.explanation: Scalar descent: stepwise descending motion through scale degrees. 8-7-6-5-4-3-2-1 traversal. Strong sense of resolution (descending toward tonic). Bach uses scalar descents in chorale bass lines and inner-voice motion. Romantic-era cantabile melodies often feature long scalar descents (Schubert lieder closing). AP Music Theory exam: recognize stepwise scalar patterns. Distinguished from arpeggiation (chord outline) and leap motion. Combined with stepwise ascent: complete melodic 'curve' from low to high to low again (or vice versa). Foundation of melodic continuity.

questions.prompt: Two notes in alternation creating repeated figure.
questions.explanation: Oscillation: alternation between two pitches. Forms basis of trills (chord tone + neighbor), tremolos (within one chord), and many ornamental figures. Can extend into broader rhythmic figures (rocking 8th notes between two notes). AP Music Theory exam: identify oscillating figures. Schubert 'Erlkönig' piano: rapid triplet oscillations between G and Bb create relentless horse-galloping effect. Wagner 'Tristan und Isolde' uses oscillating chromatic figures for tension. Different from arpeggio (which outlines chord) and from sequence (which transposes pattern). Provides driving, agitating, or contemplative effect depending on context.

questions.prompt: Composing two or more melodic lines simultaneously.
questions.explanation: Combining independent melodic lines. From 'punctus contra punctum' (point against point). Fundamental from Renaissance polyphony (Palestrina) through Baroque fugue (Bach) to 20th century. Species counterpoint (Fux 1725): 1st species (note vs note), 2nd species (2 vs 1), 3rd species (4 vs 1), 4th species (suspensions), 5th species (florid mixed). AP Music Theory Unit 6 develops these tools through Bach chorale analysis. Skills: voice independence, controlled dissonance, melodic shape. Counterpoint distinguishes from homophony (chordal accompaniment supporting one melody).

questions.prompt: Counterpoint technique where two voices have melodic motion in different rhythms.
questions.explanation: Rhythmic independence: voices have different rhythmic activity. Soprano holds long notes while bass moves in 8ths and 16ths; or vice versa. Critical to counterpoint - if rhythms identical, voices fuse into 'one' line. AP Music Theory exam: identify rhythmic independence in 4-voice texture. Bach chorales: voices have similar rhythms (4 quarter notes per measure typically) but melodic independence; in his fugues and inventions, rhythmic independence is stronger. Renaissance polyphony (Palestrina): high rhythmic independence between voices, creating shifting textures.

questions.prompt: Two voices imitating each other at a delayed time interval.
questions.explanation: Canon: strict imitation where one voice follows another exactly (in delayed entry) with same melody. Famous canons: Pachelbel's Canon in D (1680), 'Frère Jacques' (Brother John round), Bach Goldberg Variations canons. Round = canon at unison. AP Music Theory exam: identify canonic technique. Variations: canon at different intervals (canon at fifth, canon at octave), canon by inversion, canon by retrograde. Tallis 40-voice motet 'Spem in alium' has complex canonic structures. Bach Musical Offering features 10 canons demonstrating mastery.

questions.prompt: Counterpoint pattern with one voice consistently presenting same melody.
questions.explanation: Cantus firmus: 'fixed song' - given melody that other voices counterpoint around. Medieval/Renaissance practice: take Gregorian chant melody as cantus firmus, write counterpoint above and below. Fux species counterpoint exercises use simple cantus firmus melodies in long notes (whole notes) while student writes counterpoint in different species rhythms. AP Music Theory exam may reference. Bach chorale harmonization is conceptually similar: given chorale melody (in soprano typically) is the 'cantus' and bass + inner voices counterpoint around it. Foundation of Renaissance polyphonic composition.

questions.prompt: Counterpoint pattern with all voices entering on subject in turn.
questions.explanation: Fugue exposition: opening section where each voice enters in turn with the subject. Standard order: voice 1 alone (subject in tonic), voice 2 enters (answer in dominant), voice 3 (subject in tonic), voice 4 (answer in dominant), etc. Bach Well-Tempered Clavier (1722, 1742): 48 preludes and fugues showcase fugue mastery. AP Music Theory exam tests fugue identification. Subject + answer combination creates strong tonal opening. Counter-subject: independent melodic line in voices not playing subject. Episode: contrasting sections between subject statements. Fugue is pinnacle of contrapuntal art.

questions.prompt: Chord notation V/V means.
questions.explanation: V/V (V of V, 'five of five'): the V chord OF the V chord. In C major: V = G major; V/V = D major (since D is V of G). Tonicizes V temporarily. Notated V/V in Roman numeral analysis. Contains chromatic alteration: D major has F# (NOT in C major key signature). The accidental signals secondary function. AP Music Theory Unit 7 focuses extensively on secondary dominants. Most common: V/V (V of V), V/IV (V of IV), V/vi (V of vi). Used in cadential approaches: V/V-V-I creates intensified motion to V before final resolution to I.

questions.prompt: Movement from one key to another in a composition.
questions.explanation: Shift from one key to another within a composition. Establishes new tonic. Types: closely related modulation (1 accidental difference, easy), distant modulation (multiple accidental difference, harder), enharmonic modulation (using enharmonic equivalences), modulation via secondary dominants, etc. AP Music Theory Unit 7 covers modulation extensively. Most common: I to V (relative dominant; e.g., C major to G major), I to vi (relative minor; e.g., C major to A minor). Sonata form: exposition modulates I to V (or i to III in minor) for second theme group. Recapitulation returns to I.

questions.prompt: Note that creates secondary leading tone for V/V chord.
questions.explanation: Raised 4: scale degree 4 raised by half step creates secondary leading tone for V/V. In C major: raised 4 = F# (raised from F). Functions as leading tone to G (root of V). V/V = D major (D-F#-A). The F# is the 3rd of D major = leading tone of G. AP Music Theory exam: identify secondary dominants by detecting raised 4 (or raised 1, 2, etc. for other secondary dominants). Raised 4 is part of the V/V chord, indicating temporary tonicization of dominant. Voice leading: F# must resolve UP to G (leading tone behavior preserved).

questions.prompt: Function of V7/IV (dominant 7 of IV chord).
questions.explanation: V/IV (V of IV): the dominant of the subdominant. Resolves to IV. In C major: IV = F major; V/IV = C7 (C major with 7th = C-E-G-Bb). The Bb (lowered 7 of C major) signals secondary function. Common usage: tonicize IV chord briefly via V7/IV-IV motion. Pop music: I-I7-IV progression (where I7 functions as V7/IV) extremely common. Beatles 'I Saw Her Standing There' opens with I-I7-IV. AP Music Theory exam: identify V/IV by lowered 7 (Bb in C major key context).

questions.prompt: Chord progression involving secondary dominants.
choices: Up to root of V (5th of original key)
questions.explanation: V/V-V-I: intensified cadential progression. The V/V leads into V (tonicizing V momentarily), then V resolves to I. In C major: V/V = D7 (D-F#-A-C); V = G7 (G-B-D-F); I = C major. Compare to plain V-I: V/V-V-I adds extra voice-leading pull. Mozart sonatas full of these. Bach uses sparingly (more diatonic). Beethoven and Romantic-era composers use V/V-V-I and longer chains (V/V/V → V/V → V → I = double secondary dominant). AP Music Theory exam tests these chains. Modulation often signaled by secondary dominants becoming established.

questions.prompt: Two keys differing by 0 or 1 accidentals.
choices: Each tone resolves by half or whole step in specific direction
questions.explanation: Closely related keys: differ by 1 accidental (1 sharp or 1 flat away on circle of fifths). C major closely related to: G major (1 sharp), F major (1 flat), A minor (relative minor), D minor, E minor (relative minors of close major keys). Modulation between close keys is smooth - many shared chord tones, easy pivot chords. AP Music Theory exam: identify and execute close modulations. Schubert lieder often modulate to closely related keys for variety. Sonata form expositions typically modulate I to V (closely related). Distant modulation (more than 1 accidental difference) requires more elaborate technique.

questions.prompt: Modulation that uses a chord common to both keys.
choices: i to III (minor tonic to relative major)
questions.explanation: Pivot chord modulation: shared chord serves as bridge between two keys. Example: C major to G major. Common chord: D minor (ii in C major AND vi in G major). Approach in old key, reinterpret as new function, continue in new key. AP Music Theory exam tests pivot chord identification. Smoothest modulation technique. Bach uses pivot chord modulation extensively in cantatas. Mozart sonata form first-theme to second-theme transitions often via pivot chords. Distant modulations may not have natural pivot chords; require other techniques (chromatic, enharmonic).

questions.prompt: Standard sonata-form modulation in major key.
choices: Each tone resolves by half or whole step in specific direction
i to III (minor tonic to relative major)
questions.explanation: Sonata-form modulation (major key): exposition modulates from I (tonic) to V (dominant). E.g., C major sonata modulates to G major for second theme group. Reason: dominant is closely related, smooth modulation, sense of forward motion. Mozart, Haydn, Beethoven sonatas follow this pattern. In minor key: i to III (relative major) instead. AP Music Theory exam: sonata-form modulation is structural - mandatory key contrast between first and second theme groups. Recapitulation: brings back to tonic; second theme transposed to original tonic (closure).

questions.prompt: Standard sonata-form modulation in minor key.
choices: i to III (minor tonic to relative major)
Each tone resolves by half or whole step in specific direction
questions.correctText: i to III (minor tonic to relative major)
questions.explanation: Sonata-form modulation (minor key): from i (minor tonic) to III (relative major). E.g., A minor sonata modulates to C major for second theme. Reason: relative major is closely related (same key signature), provides bright contrast to minor home. Beethoven 'Pathétique' Sonata movement 1 (C minor) modulates to Eb major (III) for second theme. AP Music Theory exam tests this specifically. Alternative in minor: i to v (dominant minor) or i to V (dominant major), less common. Recapitulation in minor sonatas: second theme returned to i (or sometimes I major for triumphant ending).

questions.prompt: Voice-leading principle in resolving diminished 7th chord.
choices: Each tone resolves by half or whole step in specific direction
questions.correctText: Each tone resolves by half or whole step in specific direction
questions.explanation: Diminished 7th (vii°7) resolution: each tone resolves by step to chord tone of target. In vii°7 → I: root (leading tone) up by step; 3rd up by step; 5th down by step; 7th down by step. Symmetric stepwise convergence. AP Music Theory exam: diminished 7th has clear voice-leading rules. Smooth voice leading from vii°7 to V (or to I). Same vii°7 resolves to 4 different keys via enharmonic respelling - in each, the voice leading is the same stepwise pattern but to different target chord. Schubert and Wagner use vii°7 for dramatic modulations.

questions.prompt: Process where a new key is established by repeated emphasis.
questions.explanation: Tonicization: temporary emphasis of a chord as a 'mini tonic.' Less stable than modulation - chord receives V-I treatment briefly, then returns to original tonic. AP Music Theory exam distinguishes tonicization (brief) vs modulation (sustained establishment of new key). V/V tonicizes V; V/IV tonicizes IV; V/vi tonicizes vi. Mozart and Schubert use tonicization for color and momentary harmonic interest without committing to full modulation. Bach cantatas: rapid tonicization throughout each phrase, then return to original key. Schenkerian theory: tonicization is middleground; modulation is background.

questions.prompt: Cadence happening just before a modulation typically.
questions.explanation: Pre-modulation HC: many modulations are preceded by a half cadence (pause on V of old key), creating moment of suspended expectation before new key arrives. Example: Beethoven's Pathétique Sonata movement 1 ends second theme group with HC, then unexpected modulation begins next section. AP Music Theory exam: identify modulation entry points often marked by cadences in old key. Other entry strategies: smooth pivot (no cadence), direct/abrupt entry, surprise (deceptive cadence into new key). Modulation rhetoric: HC creates pause, anticipation, then new tonal area.

questions.prompt: Modulation type involving abrupt key change without pivot.
questions.explanation: Direct modulation: abrupt change to new key without pivot chord, usually between phrases. Old phrase ends in original key; new phrase starts in new key. Common in popular music ('truck driver's modulation' = up by half/whole step for last chorus, e.g., Whitney Houston 'I Will Always Love You'). AP Music Theory exam: identify and characterize. Classical-era: less common (preferred smoother modulations); Romantic era: more frequent. Wagner uses direct modulations dramatically. Schubert's 'Erlkönig' has tonal disruptions including direct modulations.

questions.prompt: Direction of resolution of Neapolitan 6th chord.
questions.explanation: Neapolitan 6 (N6, bII6) resolves to V. Voice leading: bass moves stepwise (F → E in C major when N6 = F-Ab-Db). Specifically, root of N6 (Db) typically resolves down by augmented 2nd (or augmented 4th) to leading tone of V chord (3rd of V). Often N6 - V7 directly, or N6 - V64 - V53 - I (cadential 6-4 elaboration). AP Music Theory exam: trace Neapolitan resolution carefully. Common in minor key cadences (where N6 is more 'native' due to flat-2 being more naturally available). Beethoven 'Moonlight' Sonata movement 1 features clear N6 resolution.

questions.prompt: Modulation using chord with multiple enharmonic spellings.
questions.explanation: Enharmonic modulation: uses a chord with the SAME pitches but DIFFERENT names in two keys. Common example: diminished 7th chord. Vii°7 has 4 enharmonic spellings each functioning as vii°7 of a different key. So one diminished 7th chord can pivot between FOUR different keys. Also: Augmented 6th chords (like Italian/French/German 6ths) enharmonically equal to dominant 7th in distant keys. AP Music Theory exam tests enharmonic modulation. Schubert and Brahms use these freely. Wagner Tristan extends enharmonic chromaticism throughout (creating 'endless melody').

questions.prompt: Modulation establishing new key by V-I cadence in new key.
questions.explanation: Authentic cadence in new key: confirms modulation by performing V-I (or V7-I) in the new key. Critical: V-I MUST be in new key (new chord roots V and I of new key). AP Music Theory exam: identifying when modulation has been established (look for full V-I cadence in new key). Modulation isn't 'real' until cadence in new key. Until then, just chromatic tones or secondary dominants. Mozart, Beethoven sonatas: clear cadential confirmation of new keys at structural points. Modulation flow: pivot chord transition + cadential confirmation.

questions.prompt: Chord built on lowered 6th scale degree in major key.
questions.explanation: BVI (flat-6): major triad on lowered scale degree 6. In C major: bVI = Ab major (Ab-C-Eb). Borrowed from C minor (where it would be VI). Modal mixture. Creates 'darkness' or 'distance' from major key. AP Music Theory exam: identify borrowed chords. Common usage: I-bVI-IV-V (sounds modal/exotic). Schubert lieder: frequent bVI for emotional color. Wagner and late Romantic: extensive borrowing. Substitutes for vi: in C major, vi = A minor; bVI = Ab major. Different chord, different mood, both pre-dominant. iii becomes bIII (Eb major in C major), iv becomes minor (F minor in C major) similarly.

questions.prompt: Chord on flat-6 with raised 4: French augmented 6th chord.
questions.explanation: French 6th (Fr6 or Fr+6): one of three augmented 6th chord types. Built on flat-6 with: root (flat-6), major 3rd, augmented 4th, augmented 6th. In C major: Fr6 = Ab-C-D-F# (flat-6, 1, 2, raised-4). The augmented 6th (Ab to F#) resolves OUTWARD: Ab down to G (5 of V), F# up to G (5 of V). Resolution to V chord. AP Music Theory exam tests augmented 6th chords. Three types: Italian 6 (3 notes), French 6 (4 notes with major 3 instead of dim 3), German 6 (4 notes with perfect 5).

questions.prompt: Chord on flat-2nd in major key.
questions.explanation: Neapolitan 6th (N6, also notated bII6): major triad built on lowered 2nd scale degree, usually in first inversion. In C major: N6 = Db major in first inversion = F-Ab-Db (bII6). Pre-dominant function: resolves to V (often via cadential 6/4). Named after Neapolitan opera composers (Scarlatti) who used it. Common in minor key (more original to that mode). Beethoven 'Moonlight' Sonata movement 1 features Neapolitan. AP Music Theory exam tests N6 identification. Voice leading: F to E (bass step down) typical. Smooth pre-dominant alternative to ii chord.

questions.prompt: Lowered 7th and 6th scale degrees forming this chord.
questions.explanation: Iv7 (subdominant minor 7th chord, also denoted iv7 in major key with borrowed quality): minor 7th chord built on scale degree 4 (borrowing minor 6 from parallel minor). In C major: iv7 = F-Ab-C-Eb (F minor 7 chord). Contains both lowered 6 (Ab) and lowered 3 of IV (Eb is normally E in F major). Strong pre-dominant function, more chromatic than diatonic ii7 or IV. Used by Schubert, Brahms, Mahler. AP Music Theory: identify borrowed chords with chromatic alterations. Resolution: standard pre-dom → V → I motion.

questions.prompt: Resolution of augmented 6th interval.
choices: Raised 4 to natural 5 (F# to G in C major)
Down by step to 3rd of I
questions.explanation: Augmented 6th resolution: expands outward to perfect octave. The aug 6th (e.g., Ab-F# in C major) resolves: Ab down half step to G; F# up half step to G. Both voices converge on G (root of V). Inverse process: diminished 3rd would CONTRACT inward to unison. AP Music Theory exam: augmented 6th MUST expand outward. Aug 6 contains 2 tendency tones (both flat-6 going down, raised-4 going up). Resolves to V or to V7. Italian, French, German 6th chords all resolve this way. Voice-leading basis of chromatic pre-dominant function.

questions.prompt: Resolution of leading tone in V chord.
choices: Raised 4 to natural 5 (F# to G in C major)
Up by half step to tonic
questions.correctText: Up by half step to tonic
questions.explanation: Leading tone (scale degree 7) resolution: UP by half step to tonic (scale degree 1). In V chord (which contains scale degree 7 as its 3rd): leading tone resolves to tonic. AP Music Theory exam tests this rigorously. Bach chorales: leading tone in outer voices ALWAYS resolves up. Inner voices: occasionally descend to 5 of I (allowed when soprano takes 3rd of I to keep complete triad). Doubling leading tone forbidden (would cause parallel resolution = parallel 8ves). Critical voice-leading principle from medieval cadential clausula vera through Schoenberg's 12-tone.

questions.prompt: Resolution of 7th in V7 chord.
choices: Down by step to 3rd of I
Up by half step to tonic
questions.correctText: Down by step to 3rd of I
questions.explanation: V7 chord 7th resolution: the 7th (scale degree 4 in major; F in C major) resolves DOWN by step to scale degree 3 (E in C major = 3rd of I). Why? 7th is a tendency tone (chordal dissonance). Strict rule: cannot leap away from chordal 7th, must descend by step. AP Music Theory exam tests this universally. Bach V7-I voice leading: F-E in soprano or inner voice. Combined with leading tone (B-C): two tendency tones resolve simultaneously creating V7-I cadential progress. 3rd of V (B) up, 7th of V (F) down.

questions.prompt: Common voice-leading pattern in V/V to V.
choices: Raised 4 to natural 5 (F# to G in C major)
Up by half step to tonic
questions.correctText: Raised 4 to natural 5 (F# to G in C major)
questions.explanation: V/V to V voice leading: raised 4 resolves up by half step to 5 (G). E.g., in C major: F# (3rd of V/V = D-F#-A) → G (root of V = G-B-D). Similar pattern to primary leading tone resolution. Other voice motion: 5th of V/V (A) - usually moves to D or B; 7th of V7/V (C) resolves down by step to B (3rd of V). AP Music Theory exam: voice leading from secondary dominant to its target. Bach uses V/V in chorales for sophisticated cadences. Romantic era: long chains of secondary dominants create rich chromatic harmony (Wagner Tristan).

questions.prompt: Chord type with three superposed minor 3rds.
choices: Down by step to 3rd of I
questions.explanation: Diminished 7th chord (vii°7): 4 notes stacked in minor 3rds: m3-m3-m3 (root-3rd-5th-7th forming d3+d5+d7). Symmetric, divides octave into equal parts. In C major: vii°7 = B-D-F-Ab. Built on leading tone with 7th. ENHARMONIC EQUIVALENCE: any diminished 7th chord has 4 enharmonic spellings, each as vii°7 of different key. So one chord = 4 potential keys. Pivot for enharmonic modulation. AP Music Theory exam tests vii°7 inversions and resolutions. Chopin, Wagner exploit enharmonic modulation through diminished 7ths. Resolve: chromatic voice leading toward target key's tonic.

questions.prompt: Mode with major scale interval pattern.
questions.explanation: Ionian mode: same as major scale. Interval pattern W-W-H-W-W-W-H. Identical to major scale of common-practice music. In white keys: C Ionian (C-D-E-F-G-A-B-C = C major). AP Music Theory exam Unit 8: modes are revival of pre-tonal church modes; Ionian was less used in medieval/Renaissance polyphony (Aeolian, Mixolydian, Dorian more common). Modern jazz, rock, folk freely use modes. Used by Renaissance polyphonists (Palestrina) and revived in 20th century by composers like Debussy, Ravel, Bartók, jazz (Miles Davis modal jazz: 'So What' uses Dorian).

questions.prompt: Mode characterized by lowered 7th compared to major.
questions.explanation: Mixolydian: W-W-H-W-W-H-W (major with lowered 7). G Mixolydian: G-A-B-C-D-E-F-G (white keys on G). Lacks leading tone (uses subtonic F instead of F#). Sounds 'dominant-tinged' (resembles V7 sonority). Examples: 'Sweet Home Alabama,' 'Norwegian Wood,' Celtic folk music, Indian raga 'Khamaj.' AP Music Theory exam: characteristic intervals and ear-training. In Renaissance music: Mixolydian common in mass and motet. Modal jazz: Mixolydian over dominant chords. Comparable: Bartok Mikrokosmos uses Mixolydian for folk-tinged pieces. G Mixolydian and G major differ by F natural vs F#.

questions.prompt: Jazz album popularizing modal approach (Miles Davis 1959).
questions.explanation: Kind of Blue (1959): Miles Davis album launching modal jazz. Avoided chord progressions in favor of single modes for extended improvisation. 'So What' = D Dorian alternating with Eb Dorian; 'All Blues' = G Mixolydian. AP Music Theory exam may reference. Players: Miles Davis (trumpet), Coltrane (tenor sax), Cannonball Adderley (alto sax), Bill Evans (piano), Paul Chambers (bass), Jimmy Cobb (drums). Best-selling jazz album ever. Sparked interest in modal scales among musicians and theorists. Earlier modal: Debussy, Bartók. Continued in Coltrane (later avant-garde), Herbie Hancock, modal-jazz-rock fusion.

questions.prompt: Composer using modal harmony extensively in impressionist style.
questions.explanation: Claude Debussy (1862-1918): French Impressionist composer. Extensively used modes (Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian), whole-tone scales, pentatonic scales, and chromatic harmony. Famous works: 'Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune' (1894), 'La Mer' (1905), 'Clair de Lune' (1890), 'Pelléas et Mélisande' (opera 1902), 24 Préludes for piano. AP Music Theory exam may reference. Influences: Russian composers (Mussorgsky), gamelan music (heard at 1889 Paris Exposition), French symbolist poetry (Verlaine, Baudelaire). Influenced jazz, film music, modern minimalism. With Ravel: led French response to German Romanticism.

questions.prompt: Mode resembling major with raised 4th.
questions.explanation: Lydian: W-W-W-H-W-W-H (major with raised 4). F Lydian: F-G-A-B-C-D-E-F (white keys on F, so the 4th is B not Bb). Raised 4 (B vs Bb in F major) creates 'floating' or 'dreamlike' sound. Examples: 'The Simpsons' theme song (Lydian), 'Maria' from West Side Story has Lydian gestures, much film music. AP Music Theory exam: Lydian by ear and notation. Modal jazz: Lydian over maj7 chords. Debussy and Ravel use Lydian heavily for impressionist 'floating' atmosphere. F Lydian and F major differ by B vs Bb.

questions.prompt: Form: A-B sections.
choices: Bass on root of V then on root of I (5-1 or down 5th up 4th)
questions.explanation: Binary form: two contrasting sections (A and B). Standard structure: A (in tonic, modulates to dominant) || B (begins in dominant, returns to tonic). Both sections typically repeated. AP Music Theory exam Unit 8: identify binary form. Used in Baroque dance movements (allemande, sarabande, gigue), Scarlatti keyboard sonatas (hundreds in binary form), Bach two-part inventions. Types: simple binary (A-B both end in different keys); rounded binary (A-B-A' with brief return); balanced binary (B has parallel material to A in different key).

questions.prompt: Form: A-B-A sections.
questions.explanation: Ternary form: three sections - A (in tonic), B (contrasting key/material), A (return to tonic with original A material). Often A-B-A' (slight variations on return). Contrast in B section can be: different key, different mood, different tempo. AP Music Theory exam tests ternary identification. Used in Classical minuet (A-B-A = minuet-trio-minuet), aria da capo (Baroque opera), Romantic character pieces (Chopin Nocturnes often ternary). Distinct from sonata form (which has development in middle, recapitulation at end). Strong cadential closure on both A statements.

questions.prompt: Form with verse-chorus alternation common in songs.
choices: Perfect 4th above bass is treated as dissonant
questions.explanation: Strophic form: same music repeats for each verse (each 'strophe' or stanza). Different text, same music. Common in folk songs ('Yankee Doodle,' 'Auld Lang Syne'), hymns ('Amazing Grace'), Schubert lieder ('Heidenröslein'). Verse-chorus variant (modified strophic): each verse music same, plus chorus refrain that may also repeat. AP Music Theory exam: identify strophic form. Schubert masterfully extends strophic form ('Erlkönig' is through-composed but many lieder are strophic). Different from through-composed (each verse different music). Pop songs often verse-chorus form.

questions.prompt: Form A-B-A-C-A-D-A-... with repeated 'A' section.
questions.explanation: Rondo form: A returns repeatedly between contrasting episodes. Pattern: A-B-A-C-A (5-part rondo) or A-B-A-C-A-D-A (7-part rondo, less common). Each return of A solidifies tonic. AP Music Theory exam tests rondo identification. Classical sonata-rondo: A is theme, B is contrasting theme often in dominant, C is sometimes development, A returns each time. Mozart Piano Sonata K. 545 finale (rondo); Beethoven 'Pathétique' finale (rondo). Final A might end with coda extending tonic. Compared to ternary: rondo has multiple returns; ternary has only one return.

questions.prompt: Form theme-statement followed by variations on that theme.
questions.explanation: Theme and variations: clear theme stated at beginning, followed by series of variations modifying it (melodic ornamentation, harmonic alteration, rhythmic transformation, mode change, etc.). Examples: Bach Goldberg Variations (theme + 30 variations + theme return), Beethoven 'Diabelli Variations' (theme + 33 variations), Brahms Variations on a Theme by Haydn. Mozart Piano Concerto K. 491 variations. AP Music Theory exam: identify theme-and-variations form. Beethoven Symphony No. 7 movement 2 is variation-form (theme + 4 variations + coda). Foundational compositional form, demonstrates compositional ingenuity.

questions.prompt: First main section of sonata form, presenting themes.
questions.explanation: Exposition: opening section of sonata form. Presents first theme group (in tonic key), bridge/transition (modulates), second theme group (in V if major key; III if minor), closing area (cadential confirmation of new key). Often repeated. AP Music Theory exam tests sonata-form sections. Mozart Symphony No. 40 movement 1: exposition presents Theme 1 in G minor, modulates, Theme 2 in Bb major (III in minor), closing. Beethoven 5th Symphony: exposition has characteristic 4-note 'fate' motive driving everything. Sonata-form is foundation of Classical and early Romantic instrumental music.

questions.prompt: Multi-movement work for orchestra.
questions.explanation: Symphony: large-scale multi-movement orchestral work. Classical 4-movement layout: I. Allegro (sonata form); II. Slow (often ternary or variations); III. Minuet-Trio (later Scherzo); IV. Allegro finale (rondo or sonata-rondo). Examples: Haydn (104 symphonies), Mozart (41), Beethoven (9, ending with vocal 'Ode to Joy'), Brahms (4), Mahler (10). AP Music Theory exam: identify symphonic form. Romantic-era symphonies expand: Beethoven 9 introduced choral voices; Berlioz Symphonie fantastique 5 movements with programmatic narrative; Mahler symphonies vast structures with complex programmatic content.

questions.prompt: Middle section of sonata form where themes are developed.
questions.explanation: Development: middle section of sonata form. Themes from exposition are developed - fragmented, sequenced, modulated through distant keys, recombined. Tonal instability and harmonic adventure. Ends with retransition (return to tonic key). AP Music Theory exam tests development section identification and analysis. Beethoven developments often the longest, most innovative section (5th Symphony movement 1 dev. is brilliant). Mozart developments more concise. Romantic-era developments expand to vast proportions (Brahms, Mahler). Themes break apart and recombine, demonstrating their constituent motives.

questions.prompt: Final main section of sonata form returning to tonic.
choices: All voices: avoid crossing (each voice has assigned range)
questions.explanation: Recapitulation: final main section of sonata form. Restates exposition themes ALL IN TONIC KEY (second theme transposed from dominant/relative back to tonic). Resolves tonal tension. Often followed by coda extending tonic. AP Music Theory exam tests recapitulation identification. Mozart Symphony No. 40 recapitulation: Theme 1 in G minor (as before), then Theme 2 ALSO in G minor (transposed from Bb). Beethoven 5th Symphony movement 1 has dramatic recapitulation. Recapitulation rebalances tonal architecture: exposition's tonal departure (I→V) is reversed in recap (V→I).

questions.prompt: Section in exposition between first and second theme groups.
questions.explanation: Transition (bridge): section in exposition connecting first theme group (in tonic) to second theme group (in dominant or relative major). Function: modulate from tonic to new key. Can present new material, develop first theme, or simply liquidate first theme. AP Music Theory exam: identify transition and its modulation. Mozart transitions often clear-cut and short; Beethoven transitions more dramatic. Closing area (codetta) follows second theme group: cadential confirmation of new key with motivic material. Sonata form expositions thus have 4 sub-sections: Theme 1, Transition, Theme 2, Codetta.

questions.prompt: Multi-movement work for solo instrument with orchestra.
questions.explanation: Concerto: multi-movement work pairing solo instrument with orchestra. Classical 3-movement: I. Allegro (sonata form with dual exposition); II. Slow; III. Rondo finale. Examples: Mozart Piano Concertos (27), Beethoven Piano Concertos (5), Brahms Violin Concerto, Tchaikovsky Piano Concerto No. 1. AP Music Theory exam tests concerto features. Concerto exposition: 'dual exposition' - orchestra plays themes in tonic, then soloist enters and develops them with orchestral support. Cadenza: virtuosic unaccompanied solo passage typically toward end of first movement, traditionally improvised.

questions.prompt: Mode characterized by lowered 2nd compared to natural minor.
questions.explanation: Phrygian: H-W-W-W-H-W-W (minor with lowered 2nd). E Phrygian: E-F-G-A-B-C-D-E (white keys on E). Lowered 2 (F♮ vs F# in E minor) is distinctive. Sounds 'Spanish' or 'Middle Eastern' due to half step at start. Flamenco music's primary mode (often E Phrygian). Examples: 'Wonderful World' bridge, 'White Rabbit' (Jefferson Airplane), much metal music. AP Music Theory exam tests Phrygian by ear and notation. Phrygian dominant (Phrygian with raised 3rd) is also common in Klezmer and Eastern European folk. In jazz: Phrygian used over m7b5 chords.

questions.prompt: Optional section after recapitulation extending tonic.
questions.explanation: Coda: 'tail' section after recapitulation. Extends and reinforces tonic key. Can be brief (4-8 measures) or extensive (Beethoven codas sometimes equal length of exposition). AP Music Theory exam: identify coda. Function: provide additional cadential confirmation, finalize tonal architecture, sometimes additional thematic development. Beethoven 5th Symphony movement 1 coda is famous (final cadence with C major chord repeated for emphasis). Mahler Symphony No. 1 has extended coda. Modern pop songs have 'outro' as coda equivalent. Coda comes from Italian 'tail.'.

questions.prompt: Form of Italian opera aria.
questions.explanation: Da capo aria: 'from the head' - return to beginning. ABA form with elaborate ornamentation on return A. Standard Baroque opera aria form (Handel Italian operas). A: thematic statement in tonic; B: contrasting (different key, mood, often minor); A: return to original A music, but performer adds improvised ornaments. AP Music Theory exam may reference Baroque opera. Handel 'Lascia ch'io pianga' from Rinaldo: classic da capo aria. Conventional in Italian opera 1700-1750. Replaced in Classical era with more varied forms. Often very long with virtuosic A return.

questions.prompt: Mode rarely used due to diminished 5th from tonic.
questions.explanation: Locrian: H-W-W-H-W-W-W (rare due to d5 from tonic). B Locrian: B-C-D-E-F-G-A-B. The tritone (B-F) from tonic to 5th makes it harmonically unstable - the i chord is diminished (B-D-F), unable to function as stable tonic. AP Music Theory exam: Locrian as theoretical mode. Mostly unused in tonal music. Some modern jazz uses it sparingly. Modern metal: occasional Locrian for dark atmosphere. Bartok Mikrokosmos has Locrian etudes. The 'rarest' church mode, treated more theoretically than practically. Other 6 modes (Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian) all have perfect 5 from tonic, allowing stable tonic chord.

questions.prompt: Music using modes for atmosphere outside common-practice tonality.
questions.explanation: Modal music: composed in modes rather than (or in combination with) common-practice major/minor tonality. Examples: Renaissance polyphony (Palestrina, Lassus), folk music (modal), jazz (Miles Davis 'Kind of Blue' modal jazz), film music (Howard Shore Lord of the Rings uses modes). AP Music Theory exam: distinguishing modal from tonal. Different harmonic syntax: modal pieces don't always use V-I cadence (might use bVII-I, or modal cadences). Debussy 'Pelléas et Mélisande' uses Dorian and Aeolian for shimmering ambiguity. Renaissance/medieval source: church modes were the standard before tonal triadic harmony emerged ~1600.

questions.prompt: Multi-movement work for solo instrument or small ensemble.
questions.explanation: Sonata: multi-movement work for solo instrument (piano sonata, violin sonata) or small ensemble (string quartet, piano trio). Classical 3-4 movements, structured similarly to symphony. Examples: Beethoven Piano Sonatas (32), Mozart Violin Sonatas, Schubert Sonatas, Brahms Cello Sonatas. AP Music Theory exam: identify sonata structure. Sonata-form (capital S) refers to first-movement structure (exposition-development-recapitulation); sonata (lowercase) refers to multi-movement work. Confusing terminology: ALL string quartets, symphonies, concertos employ sonata-form first movement, but only those named 'Sonata' are sonatas as work.

questions.prompt: Form with rondo-like return of refrain alternating with verses.
questions.explanation: Rondo + strophic hybrid: pop music verse-chorus structure adapts rondo principle (refrain returns) with strophic verses. Verse 1 - Chorus - Verse 2 - Chorus - Bridge - Chorus - Coda. Verses similar to strophic form; choruses similar to rondo refrain. AP Music Theory exam: identify hybrid forms in popular music. Many Beatles songs: 'A Day in the Life' has unique form mixing strophic and through-composed. 'Bohemian Rhapsody' (Queen) hybrid of multiple forms: ballad-strophic-operatic-hard rock-strophic-outro. Modern songwriting often syncretic - blending classical, popular, and jazz forms.

questions.prompt: Form with multiple movements telling programmatic story.
questions.explanation: Symphonic poem (tone poem): single-movement orchestral work with explicit programmatic content (telling a story, depicting a scene). Created by Liszt (1840s) with works like 'Les Préludes,' 'Mazeppa.' Followed by Strauss tone poems ('Don Juan,' 'Till Eulenspiegel,' 'Also Sprach Zarathustra'). Smetana 'The Moldau' (from Má vlast). AP Music Theory exam may reference. Single-movement structure contrasts with multi-movement symphony; programmatic content distinguishes from 'absolute' (non-programmatic) instrumental music. Liszt invented the genre; Strauss elevated it to peak. Modern descendants: film scores often function like symphonic poems.

questions.prompt: Form prevalent in 20th-century experimental music with no traditional structure.
questions.explanation: Through-composed: new musical material continuously, no significant repetition or return. Each section is unique. AP Music Theory exam contrasts with strophic, ternary, rondo. Examples: Schubert 'Erlkönig' (through-composed Lied; each verse different music as story unfolds dramatically); Wagner operas (continuous music, 'endless melody'); much 20th-century music. Distinct from form-based composition. Modern experimental music (Stockhausen, Cage) often through-composed or 'free form.' Pop ballads occasionally through-composed: Bohemian Rhapsody. Folk-style songs typically strophic; art music often through-composed for textual responsiveness.

questions.prompt: Interval of 12 half steps.
questions.explanation: Octave: 12 half steps (semitones). Frequency ratio 2:1 (e.g., A440 to A880). Pitch class repeats at octave: C, then next octave's C is same pitch class but doubled frequency. AP Music Theory exam fundamental. Foundation of pitch organization: 12 semitones per octave in equal temperament. Octave displacement: same pitch class at different octaves. Octave equivalence: musical convention treating octave-related pitches as 'same.' Western music since Pythagoras based on octave equivalence. Most consonant interval after unison.

questions.prompt: Function of the IV chord.
questions.explanation: IV: subdominant or pre-dominant function. Built on scale degree 4. Prepares V. In C major: IV = F-A-C. AP Music Theory exam tests chord function: tonic (I, vi as tonic substitute), pre-dominant (ii, IV), dominant (V, vii°), and back to tonic. T-PD-D-T standard progression. IV and ii share scale degree 4 as their common note, making them functionally interchangeable. Subdominant prolongation: extending PD function through multiple chords (IV-ii6-IV-V, etc.). Used everywhere in Bach chorales and Mozart sonatas.

questions.prompt: Number of sharps in B major.
questions.explanation: B major: 5 sharps - F♯, C♯, G♯, D♯, A♯. To find: order of sharps F#C#G#D#A#E#B# - take first 5 = 5 sharps. Penultimate sharp (D#) + half step = E... wait. Let me redo: penultimate sharp + half step = key, so if D# is penultimate, key is E. But B major has 5 sharps. Order: 1#=G, 2#=D, 3#=A, 4#=E, 5#=B, 6#=F#, 7#=C#. Yes, 5 sharps = B major. Examples: Chopin Nocturne in B major Op. 32 No. 1. AP Music Theory exam: rapid identification of key signatures. Order of sharps: F C G D A E B (mnemonic 'Father Charles Goes Down And Ends Battle').

questions.prompt: Quality of a 4th from C to F.
questions.explanation: C to F: Perfect fourth (P4) = 5 half steps. C-D-E-F: 4 letter names, 5 semitones. P4 inverts to P5. Frequency ratio 4:3 (Pythagorean tuning). AP Music Theory exam: interval identification. Important: P4 above bass treated as DISSONANT in counterpoint (e.g., 6/4 chords); but P4 between non-bass voices is consonant. Augmented 4th (C-F#, 6 hs, tritone): different from P4. Half-step difference between perfect and augmented. Perfect intervals: 1st, 4th, 5th, 8th.

questions.prompt: Mode if you play white keys from D to D.
questions.explanation: D Dorian: D-E-F-G-A-B-C-D (white keys starting on D). Differs from D natural minor by raised 6th (B♮ instead of B♭). Distinctive 'minor with raised 6th' sound. Symmetric pattern: W-H-W-W-W-H-W (read both directions same). Examples: 'Scarborough Fair,' Miles Davis 'So What.' AP Music Theory exam tests modes (especially Unit 8). Other modes starting on white keys: C Ionian (major), E Phrygian, F Lydian, G Mixolydian, A Aeolian (natural minor), B Locrian. Memorizing white-key mode starts is essential study tool.

questions.prompt: Type of cadence V-I in root position with tonic in soprano.
choices: iv6 to V in minor key with bass half step down
questions.explanation: Perfect Authentic Cadence (PAC): V-I (or V7-I) where both chords in root position AND soprano on tonic (scale degree 1) at I chord. Strongest cadential closure. Distinguished from Imperfect Authentic Cadence (IAC) which lacks one of these strictness conditions. AP Music Theory exam: PAC vs IAC distinction is foundational. Bach chorales typically end every chorale with PAC; phrases often end IAC or HC mid-piece. Beethoven 5th Symphony final cadence: extreme PAC reinforcement (Cm-G7-Cm-Cm-Cm-Cm to absolutely confirm C minor).

questions.prompt: What is a Phrygian half cadence.
choices: iv6 to V in minor key with bass half step down
questions.correctText: iv6 to V in minor key with bass half step down
questions.explanation: Phrygian half cadence: iv6 to V in minor key. Bass descends by half step: F to E (in A minor where iv6 = F-A-D, then V = E-G#-B). Characteristic Baroque cadential figure with mournful, archaic quality. Named after Phrygian mode's distinctive half-step on top of fourth degree. AP Music Theory exam: identify Phrygian HC specifically. Common in Vivaldi slow movements, Bach cantatas, Pachelbel Canon. Beethoven uses occasionally for archaic touch. Distinguished from regular half cadence by iv6 (not ii or IV in normal positions) and characteristic bass half-step descent.

questions.prompt: Composer of Tristan und Isolde with chromatic harmony.
choices: iv6 to V in minor key with bass half step down
Numerous pop songs - 'Let It Be,' 'Don't Stop Believin'
questions.explanation: Wagner (1813-1883): German Romantic composer. Tristan und Isolde (1859, premiered 1865) opens with the 'Tristan chord' (F-B-D#-G#) - half-diminished 7th with delayed resolution. Establishes 'endless melody' and extreme chromaticism. AP Music Theory exam: Wagner exemplifies late Romantic chromatic harmony pushing toward atonal music. Other Wagner: Der Ring des Nibelungen (4-opera cycle), Tannhäuser, Lohengrin, Parsifal. Leitmotif technique: recurring musical themes associated with characters/concepts. Influenced Mahler, Strauss, Schoenberg. 'Music of the future' (his term) for total artwork (Gesamtkunstwerk).

questions.prompt: Allowed parallel intervals.
questions.explanation: Parallel 3rds and 6ths: permitted (and beautiful) in tonal music. Imperfect consonances - retain voice independence even in parallel motion. Common in Romantic harmony for 'sweet' effect (Schubert songs). Inversions of each other: 3rd inverts to 6th. AP Music Theory exam: distinguish parallel 3rds/6ths (allowed) from parallel 5ths/8ves (forbidden). Bach chorales feature parallel 3rds and 6ths between adjacent voices (S-A or T-B). Parallel 4ths between non-bass voices also generally OK; parallel 4ths involving bass are problematic (4th against bass dissonant).

questions.prompt: Composer of 'Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune' in impressionist style.
questions.explanation: Debussy (1862-1918): French Impressionist composer. 'Prélude à l'après-midi d'un faune' (1894) opens with chromatic flute solo, modal harmonies, no clear tonic. Avoided functional tonal harmony. Used modes (Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian), whole-tone scales, pentatonic scales, parallel 5ths, ambiguous tonality. AP Music Theory exam: Debussy exemplifies Impressionist break from common-practice tonality. Other Debussy: La Mer (1905), Pelléas et Mélisande (opera 1902), 24 Préludes for piano, Children's Corner. Influences: Russian music (Mussorgsky), gamelan (1889 Paris Exposition), French symbolist poetry.

questions.prompt: Voice leading rule for leading tone in outer voices.
choices: Must resolve up by half step to tonic
questions.correctText: Must resolve up by half step to tonic
questions.explanation: Leading tone resolution: must ascend half step to tonic in OUTER voices (soprano or bass). Inner voices (alto, tenor) have some flexibility (can descend to 5 of I if soprano takes 3rd of I). Doubling leading tone forbidden (parallel resolution = parallel 8ves). AP Music Theory exam rigorously tests. Bach chorales: no exceptions in soprano or bass; occasional inner-voice exceptions. Critical for proper V-I or V7-I voice leading. Leading tone is scale degree 7 in major key or harmonic/melodic minor.

questions.prompt: Parallel intervals strictly forbidden in counterpoint.
choices: Must resolve up by half step to tonic
questions.explanation: Forbidden parallels: parallel perfect fifths (P5) and parallel perfect octaves (P8). Diagnostic: compare intervals between same two voices in consecutive chords. If both are P5 or both P8, parallel error. AP Music Theory exam: most common voice-leading error to identify. Bach chorales: zero parallels (occasional voice-exchange tolerated). Hidden 5ths/8ves (similar motion to P5/P8 with leap in outer voice) also avoided. Solution: change voice (use first inversion), substitute chord, redirect motion. Modern non-tonal music freely uses parallel 5ths as deliberate effect.

questions.prompt: Notes of the V7 chord in F major.
choices: Must resolve up by half step to tonic
Each tone resolves by half or whole step in specific direction
questions.explanation: V7 in F major: built on scale degree 5 (C) with 7th. C major triad + flat 7 = C-E-G-Bb. Quality: dominant 7th chord (M3 + P5 + m7 from root). In jazz notation: C7. Voice leading to I (F major: F-A-C-F): E (3rd of V7 = leading tone) up to F; Bb (7th of V7) down to A (3rd of I); G (5th of V7) down to F (root of I); C (root of V7) up to C (5th of I, or stays as bass moves to F). AP Music Theory exam fundamental: building V7 in any key and resolving to I.

questions.prompt: Resolution direction of 7th in V7 chord.
choices: Must resolve up by half step to tonic
questions.explanation: V7 7th resolution: down by step. In C major V7 = G-B-D-F; the F (the 7th, scale degree 4) resolves DOWN by step to E (3rd of I). Universal rule for chordal 7ths. AP Music Theory exam tests rigorously. Bach chorale V7-I: 7th descends stepwise; leading tone (B) ascends to C. Both tendency tones resolve simultaneously. Failure to resolve 7th down is a typical voice-leading error. Some exceptions exist (transferred resolution where 7th goes elsewhere, register changes), but default is descend by step.

questions.prompt: What is V/V in C major.
questions.explanation: V/V in C major: dominant of dominant = D major (D-F#-A, or D7 = D-F#-A-C). Tonicizes V (G major) momentarily. Contains F#, the secondary leading tone of G. Voice leading: F# resolves UP to G (root of V), then V resolves to I. AP Music Theory exam: identify secondary dominants by raised 4 (or other chromatic alteration). V/V common in cadential approach: V/V-V-I creates intensified motion to V before final resolution to I.

questions.prompt: Mode of natural minor scale.
questions.explanation: Aeolian mode: identical to natural minor scale. White keys A to A: A-B-C-D-E-F-G-A. In any key: scale = relative major starting on its 6th degree. AP Music Theory exam: Aeolian = natural minor. Distinguished from harmonic minor (raised 7) and melodic minor (raised 6 and 7 ascending). Aeolian and minor key context: Aeolian mode tends to KEEP natural 7 throughout (subtonic); minor key with cadences raises 7 to leading tone via harmonic/melodic. Examples: 'House of the Rising Sun' (Aeolian on A), Renaissance polyphony (used Aeolian frequently).

questions.prompt: Form A-B-A.
questions.explanation: Ternary form: three sections, A-B-A. A in tonic; B contrasts (different key, mood, material); A returns to tonic, often with embellishments (A'). AP Music Theory exam: identify ternary. Used in Classical minuet (Minuet-Trio-Minuet = ternary), Baroque aria da capo, Romantic character pieces (Chopin Nocturnes), folk songs. Compared to binary (A-B, no return) and rondo (A-B-A-C-A, multiple returns). Strong cadential closure both A statements: 'home-away-home' structure.

questions.prompt: Form A-B-A-C-A with refrain returns.
questions.explanation: Rondo form: refrain A returns alternately with contrasting episodes B and C. Standard 5-part rondo: A-B-A-C-A. 7-part rondo possible (A-B-A-C-A-D-A). Each A return in tonic. Episodes typically modulate to related keys. AP Music Theory exam: identify rondo by recurring A. Classical sonata-rondo: A = theme (in I), B in V (returning to I), C may be development, A returns. Mozart Piano Sonata K. 545 finale; Beethoven 'Pathétique' Sonata finale. Final A might extend into coda for closure.

questions.prompt: Three sections of sonata form.
choices: Each tone resolves by half or whole step in specific direction
questions.explanation: Sonata form sections: (1) Exposition - presents Theme 1 (tonic), Bridge (modulates), Theme 2 (dominant or III), Closing area. Often repeated. (2) Development - themes developed in various keys; tonal instability; retransition to tonic. (3) Recapitulation - Theme 1 restated (tonic), Bridge (no modulation), Theme 2 transposed to tonic, Closing area in tonic. Optional Coda extends tonic. AP Music Theory exam: identify all three sections and their tonal plan. Mozart Symphony No. 40, Beethoven 5th Symphony, Haydn 'Surprise' Symphony all have classic sonata form first movements.

questions.prompt: Multi-movement work for solo instrument + orchestra.
questions.explanation: Concerto: multi-movement work pairing soloist with orchestra. Classical 3-movement: I. Allegro (sonata form with dual exposition); II. Slow; III. Rondo finale. AP Music Theory exam tests concerto identification. Mozart Piano Concertos (27), Beethoven Piano Concertos (5), Brahms Violin Concerto. Dual exposition: orchestra plays themes in tonic, then soloist enters and develops. Cadenza: virtuosic unaccompanied solo passage. Concerto Grosso (Baroque): small group (concertino) alternating with full orchestra (ripieno) - Bach Brandenburg Concertos. Solo Concerto (Classical/Romantic): one soloist with orchestra.

questions.prompt: Two voices moving in opposite directions.
questions.explanation: Contrary motion: voices move in opposite directions (one up, other down). Maximizes voice independence. Best motion in tonal counterpoint. Automatically avoids parallel 5ths/8ves. AP Music Theory exam: identify and prefer contrary motion. Bach chorales: ~30-40% contrary motion. Fux's species counterpoint ranks motion types: contrary > oblique > similar > parallel (with restrictions). Specifically critical in outer voices (soprano and bass). PAC cadence: bass moves V→I (down 5th); soprano often 7→1 (up half step) - contrary motion.

questions.prompt: Composer of the 371 chorales studied as model voice leading.
questions.explanation: J.S. Bach (1685-1750): German Baroque composer. 371 four-part chorale harmonizations published posthumously (Riemenschneider 1941) are pedagogical model for tonal voice leading. Also: Brandenburg Concertos, Well-Tempered Clavier (48 preludes and fugues in all 24 keys), Goldberg Variations, B Minor Mass, St. Matthew Passion, 200+ Cantatas, Toccatas, Inventions, Fugues. AP Music Theory exam: Bach is paramount reference for tonal composition. Studied by Mozart, Beethoven, Brahms, Schoenberg as model. Demonstrates all voice-leading principles in tightest 4-voice texture.

questions.prompt: Composer of Symphony No. 40 in G minor.
questions.explanation: Mozart (1756-1791): Austrian Classical composer. Symphony No. 40 in G minor (K. 550, 1788) is one of two symphonies in minor keys. Opens with famous melancholic theme G-G-G-Eb in violins. AP Music Theory exam references Mozart frequently for sonata-form examples. Other Mozart: 41 symphonies, 27 piano concertos, 23 string quartets, operas (Don Giovanni, Magic Flute, Marriage of Figaro), Requiem (incomplete at death). Mastered sonata form, sonata-rondo, theme and variations. Eine kleine Nachtmusik (K. 525) is paradigmatic Classical-era serenade in 4 movements.

questions.prompt: Composer of Symphony No. 5 in C minor.
questions.explanation: Beethoven (1770-1827): German Classical-to-Romantic transition composer. Symphony No. 5 in C minor (Op. 67, 1808) features iconic 'fate motive' (G-G-G-Eb, short-short-short-long). Movement 4 transitions C minor to triumphant C major. AP Music Theory exam: Beethoven exemplifies expanded sonata form, motivic development, and dramatic harmonic progression. Other Beethoven: 32 piano sonatas, 16 string quartets, 5 piano concertos, 9 symphonies (final with 'Ode to Joy' chorus). Late Beethoven (Op. 130+ string quartets) pushes Classical conventions. Bridges Classical (Mozart, Haydn) to Romantic (Schubert, Wagner).

questions.prompt: Note typically doubled in I chord.
questions.explanation: Doubling in I chord: ROOT most common. In C major I: typical voicing has 2 C's (root doubled), 1 E, 1 G. Doubling 5th OK but less common. AVOID doubling 3rd in major chord (could cause parallel issues). AP Music Theory exam: doubling rules - never double leading tone (in V chord); double root or 5th typically. In V chord: don't double 3rd (leading tone). In vii° (diminished): don't double root (would emphasize tritone) - double 3rd or 5th instead. In V7: usually complete chord, no doubling needed. Specific rules per Roman numeral chord function.
